%0 Conference Paper
%F Oral 
%T Challenges in Enabling Industrial End-Users to use AR and VR for 3D Authoring of Digital Twins for the Factory of the Future
%+ Equipe Immersive Natural User Interaction team (Lab-STICC_INUIT)
%+ Département Informatique (IMT Atlantique - INFO)
%A Cunin, Clémence
%A Duval, Thierry
%A Fleury, Cédric
%Z Projet 5G Metaverse, BPI France, PIA4
%< avec comité de lecture
%B 4th IEEE VR 2025 Workshop on 3D Reconstruction, Digital Twinning, and Simulation for Virtual Experiences
%C Saint-Malo, France
%8 2025-03-09
%D 2025
%K Human-centered computing → Mixed / augmented reality Virtual reality Collaborative interaction Collaborative content creation Computer supported cooperative work Ubiquitous and mobile computing systems and tools
%K Human-centered computing → Mixed / augmented reality
%K Virtual reality
%K Collaborative interaction
%K Collaborative content creation
%K Computer supported cooperative work
%K Ubiquitous and mobile computing systems and tools
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR]
%Z Computer Science [cs]
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]
%Z Engineering Sciences [physics]Conference papers
%X In this position paper, we present a brief literature overview about the authoring of digital twins in augmented and virtual reality, with a focus on how they can be used by industrial end-users for three common 3D authoring tasks: mesh segmentation, positioning, and behavior creation. This literature overview shows that most tools are lacking some features that are useful and necessary in this context. These features are (i) being cross-platform, (ii) being easy to use by industrial end-users, and (iii) being able to support asynchronous and synchronous collaboration between several users. Last, we detail shortly how our future work will focus on developing a collaborative authoring tool that will enable several industrial end-users to collaborate in an asymmetric way to achieve the three authoring features identified in the literature overview.
%G English

abstract_s: In this position paper, we present a brief literature overview about the authoring of digital twins in augmented and virtual reality, with a focus on how they can be used by industrial end-users for three common 3D authoring tasks: mesh segmentation, positioning, and behavior creation. This literature overview shows that most tools are lacking some features that are useful and necessary in this context. These features are (i) being cross-platform, (ii) being easy to use by industrial end-users, and (iii) being able to support asynchronous and synchronous collaboration between several users. en_abstract_s: In this position paper, we present a brief literature overview about the authoring of digital twins in augmented and virtual reality, with a focus on how they can be used by industrial end-users for three common 3D authoring tasks: mesh segmentation, positioning, and behavior creation. This literature overview shows that most tools are lacking some features that are useful and necessary in this context. These features are (i) being cross-platform, (ii) being easy to use by industrial end-users, and (iii) being able to support asynchronous and synchronous collaboration between several users. Last, we detail shortly how our future work will focus on developing a collaborative authoring tool that will enable several industrial end-users to collaborate in an asymmetric way to achieve the three authoring features identified in the literature overview.

In this position paper, we present a brief literature overview about the authoring of digital twins in augmented and virtual reality, with a focus on how they can be used by industrial end-users for three common 3D authoring tasks: mesh segmentation, positioning, and behavior creation. This literature overview shows that most tools are lacking some features that are useful and necessary in this context. These features are (i) being cross-platform, (ii) being easy to use by industrial end-users, and (iii) being able to support asynchronous and synchronous collaboration between several users. Last, we detail shortly how our future work will focus on developing a collaborative authoring tool that will enable several industrial end-users to collaborate in an asymmetric way to achieve the three authoring features identified in the literature overview.

%0 Conference Proceedings %T Mobilité des personnes avec une déficience intellectuelle : vers l'intégration de la collaboration dans un système d'aide à la navigation %+ Laboratoire d'Automatique, de Mécanique et d'Informatique industrielles et Humaines - UMR 8201 (LAMIH) %A Bault, Constance %A Lepreux, Sophie %A Marçal de Oliveira, Káthia %A Kolski, Christophe %< avec comité de lecture %B JCJC 2025 - Colloque Jeunes Chercheuses Jeunes Chercheurs - Technologies, Insertion, Handicap, Autonomie, Vieillissement %C Aubervilliers, France %8 2025-06-10 %D 2025 %K Technologie d'assistance %K Mobilité %K Déficience intellectuelle %Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Conference papers %X

Les personnes avec Déficience Intellectuelle (DI) peuvent éprouver des difficultés plus ou moins importantes dans leur vie quotidienne et notamment dans leurs déplacements, en fonction de leur déficience et de leurs capacités. Or, la mobilité est primordiale pour une participation sociale satisfaisante. Des systèmes d'aide à la navigation sont proposés, mais lorsque les personnes avec DI rencontrent un imprévu, la plupart d'entre elles ne savent pas comment réagir. La contribution attendue pour ce travail vise à répondre à cette problématique et à proposer un outil permettant à la personne avec déficience intellectuelle d'être en lien avec une autre personne pour l'aider. L'élaboration de ce système suivra une démarche de conception centrée utilisateur. Ce papier présente le contexte, la problématique, ainsi qu'une proposition de méthodologie qui sera utilisée pour y répondre.

Colloque Jeunes Chercheuses Jeunes Chercheurs - Technologies, Insertion, Handicap, Autonomie, Vieillissement 2025-06-10 2025-06-10 Aubervilliers France conferenceOrganizer IFRATH 2025-06-10 French Technologie d'assistance Mobilité Déficience intellectuelle Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

<div><p>Les personnes avec Déficience Intellectuelle (DI) peuvent éprouver des difficultés plus ou moins importantes dans leur vie quotidienne et notamment dans leurs déplacements, en fonction de leur déficience et de leurs capacités. Or, la mobilité est primordiale pour une participation sociale satisfaisante. Des systèmes d'aide à la navigation sont proposés, mais lorsque les personnes avec DI rencontrent un imprévu, la plupart d'entre elles ne savent pas comment réagir. La contribution attendue pour ce travail vise à répondre à cette problématique et à proposer un outil permettant à la personne avec déficience intellectuelle d'être en lien avec une autre personne pour l'aider. L'élaboration de ce système suivra une démarche de conception centrée utilisateur. Ce papier présente le contexte, la problématique, ainsi qu'une proposition de méthodologie qui sera utilisée pour y répondre.</p></div>

%T Towards a Dynamic Model of Collective Intelligence: Theoretical Integration, Nonverbal Interaction and Temporality
%+ Laboratoire d'Innovation Numérique pour les Entreprises et les Apprentissages au service de la Compétitivité des Territoires (LINEACT)
%A Lannuzel, Tristan
%A Biancardi, Beatrice
%A Barange, Mukesh
%A Buisine, Stéphanie
%< avec comité de lecture

%C San Diego CA USA, United States
%I ACM
%P 129-138
%8 2025-08-04
%D 2025
%R 10.1145/3715928.3737468
%Z Cognitive scienceConference papers
%X Most existing research on Collective Intelligence (CI) tends to emphasize final performance indicators or sums of individual cognitive traits, giving insufficient attention to how teams dynamically construct their collective capacity through ongoing interactions. In this paper, we propose an integrative perspective that draws on multiple existing approaches, ranging from conceptual frameworks (IMOI, TSM-CI) to measurement-oriented constructs (C-factor, synergy) and team cognition, while underscoring the essential behavioral and temporal processes that drive collective intelligence. Rather than viewing collective intelligence as a static end-point, our model explores how nonverbal cues (e.g., posture, gaze, interpersonal distance), repeated feedback loops, and macro-level temporal dynamics converge to shape a team’s coordination, cohesion, and shared use of knowledge. By highlighting the continuous, real-time mechanisms through which teams adapt, self-regulate, and refine their performance, we address the need for a more process-oriented, longitudinal perspective. We also propose practical methodological pathways to more accurately capture how teams evolve from loosely connected individuals into high-performing collectives over time.

en_abstract_s: Most existing research on Collective Intelligence (CI) tends to emphasize final performance indicators or sums of individual cognitive traits, giving insufficient attention to how teams dynamically construct their collective capacity through ongoing interactions. In this paper, we propose an integrative perspective that draws on multiple existing approaches, ranging from conceptual frameworks (IMOI, TSM-CI) to measurement-oriented constructs (C-factor, synergy) and team cognition, while underscoring the essential behavioral and temporal processes that drive collective intelligence. Rather than viewing collective intelligence as a static end-point, our model explores how nonverbal cues (e.g., posture, gaze, interpersonal distance), repeated feedback loops, and macro-level temporal dynamics converge to shape a team’s coordination, cohesion, and shared use of knowledge. By highlighting the continuous, real-time mechanisms through which teams adapt, self-regulate, and refine their performance, we address the need for a more process-oriented, longitudinal perspective. We also propose practical methodological pathways to more accurately capture how teams evolve from loosely connected individuals into high-performing collectives over time.

Attribution ANR Hesam CESI : groupe d’Enseignement Supérieur et de Formation Professionnelle - Hesam Groupe d’Enseignement Supérieur et de Formation Professionnelle CESI - GROUPE D'ENSEIGNEMENT SUPERIEUR ET DE FORMATION PROFESSIONNELLE PEPR eNSEMBLE International No No Yes No Towards a Dynamic Model of Collective Intelligence: Theoretical Integration, Nonverbal Interaction and Temporality Tristan Lannuzel 2771cae4b6b27e87ee19ecc9c3adbed4 cesi.fr tristan-lannuzel 1521271 3386815-1521271 https://orcid.org/0009-0002-3392-5838 Beatrice Biancardi f0c847638473329e3a1e1f15932caba5 telecom-paris.fr beatrice-biancardi 1251583 1208394-1251583 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6664-6117 https://www.idref.fr/245406417 https://viaf.org/viaf/79159697811703312560 Mukesh Barange b32af52376ca9ecbfa4f9379a6919054 cesi.fr mbarange 1338952 11562-1338952 https://www.idref.fr/23373399X https://orcid.org/0009-0006-2490-5893 Stéphanie Buisine df9e7c4fef7699f74092dea52cba1613 ensam.eu stephanie-buisine 1251097 468715-1251097 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1389-570X https://www.idref.fr/095620400 https://viaf.org/viaf/211997942 CI 2025: ACM Collective Intelligence Conference 2025-08-04 San Diego CA USA United States ACM 129-138 10.1145/3715928.3737468 English Cognitive science Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Most existing research on Collective Intelligence (CI) tends to emphasize final performance indicators or sums of individual cognitive traits, giving insufficient attention to how teams dynamically construct their collective capacity through ongoing interactions. In this paper, we propose an integrative perspective that draws on multiple existing approaches, ranging from conceptual frameworks (IMOI, TSM-CI) to measurement-oriented constructs (C-factor, synergy) and team cognition, while underscoring the essential behavioral and temporal processes that drive collective intelligence. Rather than viewing collective intelligence as a static end-point, our model explores how nonverbal cues (e.g., posture, gaze, interpersonal distance), repeated feedback loops, and macro-level temporal dynamics converge to shape a team’s coordination, cohesion, and shared use of knowledge. By highlighting the continuous, real-time mechanisms through which teams adapt, self-regulate, and refine their performance, we address the need for a more process-oriented, longitudinal perspective. We also propose practical methodological pathways to more accurately capture how teams evolve from loosely connected individuals into high-performing collectives over time.

%0 Journal Article
%T Sense of agency in cooperative gestures: The effect of hierarchical roles
%+ Interacting with Large Data - LISN (ILDA)
%+ Architectures et Modèles pour l'Interaction - LISN (AMI)
%A Zheng, Xinpei
%A Chapuis, Olivier
%A Grynszpan, Ouriel
%< avec comité de lecture
%@ 1071-5819
%J International Journal of Human-Computer Studies
%I Elsevier
%V 211
%P 103803
%8 2026-04
%D 2026
%R 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2026.103803
%K Cooperative gestures
%K Tabletops
%K Intentional Binding
%K Sense of Agency
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]
%Z Cognitive science/Computer scienceJournal articles
%X Cooperative gestures are gestures performed by multiple users to facilitate collaboration on large interactive surfaces, such as tabletops. While previous research suggested that such gestures enhance user experience, they overlooked important aspects of user experience and, in particular, the users' sense of agency. This paper considers two types of cooperative gestures: (i) egalitarian, where both users have the same role, and (ii) hierarchical, where one user (the leader) leads the action, and the other user (the follower) follows the leader's actions. In a user experiment, we evaluate these types of cooperative gestures by measuring the participants' sense of agency using intentional binding and judgment of control. Results show a decrease in the sense of agency for the followers. By contrast, leaders and users with egalitarian roles maintained their agency at the implicit sensorimotor level. We conclude with "implications for design" of these findings.

title_s: Sense of agency in cooperative gestures: The effect of hierarchical roles
abstract_s: Cooperative gestures are gestures performed by multiple users to facilitate collaboration on large interactive surfaces, such as tabletops. While previous research suggested that such gestures enhance user experience, they overlooked important aspects of user experience and, in particular, the users' sense of agency. This paper considers two types of cooperative gestures: (i) egalitarian, where both users have the same role, and (ii) hierarchical, where one user (the leader) leads the action, and the other user (the follower) follows the leader's actions. In a user experiment, we evaluate these types of cooperative gestures by measuring the participants' sense of agency using intentional binding and judgment of control. Results show a decrease in the sense of agency for the followers. By contrast, leaders and users with egalitarian roles maintained their agency at the implicit sensorimotor level. We conclude with "implications for design" of these findings.
en_abstract_s: Cooperative gestures are gestures performed by multiple users to facilitate collaboration on large interactive surfaces, such as tabletops. While previous research suggested that such gestures enhance user experience, they overlooked important aspects of user experience and, in particular, the users' sense of agency. This paper considers two types of cooperative gestures: (i) egalitarian, where both users have the same role, and (ii) hierarchical, where one user (the leader) leads the action, and the other user (the follower) follows the leader's actions. In a user experiment, we evaluate these types of cooperative gestures by measuring the participants' sense of agency using intentional binding and judgment of control. Results show a decrease in the sense of agency for the followers. By contrast, leaders and users with egalitarian roles maintained their agency at the implicit sensorimotor level. We conclude with "implications for design" of these findings.

 Sense of agency in cooperative gestures: The effect of hierarchical roles Xinpei Zheng 6fd6509ff12e24930acf7216f29c7c36 universite-paris-saclay.fr xinpei-zheng 1664114 4086843-1664114 https://orcid.org/0009-0007-4620-6441 Olivier Chapuis 6b2e85c13f6502b1f208ca753d4bc8e5 lri.fr olivier-chapuis 110 14291-110 https://www.idref.fr/18584605X https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0632-3700 Ouriel Grynszpan 712701376e45ea485288b5c76156f627 upmc.fr ourielgrynszpan 1106199 690248-1106199 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7141-5946 https://www.idref.fr/102749299 http://isni.org/isni/0000000357523980 https://viaf.org/viaf/197181172 Olivier Chapuis 6b2e85c13f6502b1f208ca753d4bc8e5 lri.fr 2026-04-01 21:48:51 2026-04-23 12:03:45 2026-04-03 14:02:06 2026-04 2026-04-01 contributor Olivier Chapuis 6b2e85c13f6502b1f208ca753d4bc8e5 lri.fr CCSD hal-05576663 https://hal.science/hal-05576663 zheng:hal-05576663 <i>International Journal of Human-Computer Studies</i>, 2026, 211, pp.103803. <a target="_blank" href="https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhcs.2026.103803">⟨10.1016/j.ijhcs.2026.103803⟩</a> International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 2026, 211, pp.103803. ⟨10.1016/j.ijhcs.2026.103803⟩ CC BY 4.0 - Attribution CNRS - Centre national de la recherche scientifique INRIA - Institut National de Recherche en Informatique et en Automatique INRIA Saclay - Ile de France INRIA - Institut National de Recherche en Informatique et en Automatique TESTALAIN1 Ecole CentraleSupélec INRIA 2 Université Paris-Saclay Université Paris-Saclay ANR Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire des Sciences du Numérique Graduate School Computer Science Interacting with Large Data Architectures et Modèles pour l'Interaction PEPR eNSEMBLE Continuité collaborative du numérique vers l'humain International No Yes Sense of agency in cooperative gestures: The effect of hierarchical roles Xinpei Zheng 6fd6509ff12e24930acf7216f29c7c36 universite-paris-saclay.fr xinpei-zheng 1664114 4086843-1664114 https://orcid.org/0009-0007-4620-6441 Olivier Chapuis 6b2e85c13f6502b1f208ca753d4bc8e5 lri.fr olivier-chapuis 110 14291-110 https://www.idref.fr/18584605X https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0632-3700 Ouriel Grynszpan 712701376e45ea485288b5c76156f627 upmc.fr ourielgrynszpan 1106199 690248-1106199 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7141-5946 https://www.idref.fr/102749299 http://isni.org/isni/0000000357523980 https://viaf.org/viaf/197181172 5624 1071-5819 1095-9300 International Journal of Human-Computer Studies Elsevier 211 103803 2026-04 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2026.103803 English Cooperative gestures Tabletops Intentional Binding Sense of Agency Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Cognitive science/Computer science Journal articles Journal articles Journal articles

Cooperative gestures are gestures performed by multiple users to facilitate collaboration on large interactive surfaces, such as tabletops. While previous research suggested that such gestures enhance user experience, they overlooked important aspects of user experience and, in particular, the users' sense of agency. This paper considers two types of cooperative gestures: (i) egalitarian, where both users have the same role, and (ii) hierarchical, where one user (the leader) leads the action, and the other user (the follower) follows the leader's actions. In a user experiment, we evaluate these types of cooperative gestures by measuring the participants' sense of agency using intentional binding and judgment of control. Results show a decrease in the sense of agency for the followers. By contrast, leaders and users with egalitarian roles maintained their agency at the implicit sensorimotor level. We conclude with "implications for design" of these findings.

%0 Conference Paper
%F Oral 
%T Sustaining the Experience of Pair Natural Walking in Social Virtual Reality
%+ Perception, Action, Cognition pour la Conception et l’Ergonomie (LS2N - équipe PACCE)
%+ Laboratoire des Sciences du Numérique de Nantes (LS2N)
%+ Nantes Université - Ecole Polytechnique de l'Université de Nantes (Nantes Univ - EPUN)
%+ Nantes Université (Nantes Univ)
%+ Nantes Université - UFR Faculté des Langues et Cultures Etrangères (Nantes Univ - UFR FLCE)
%+ NANTES UNIVERSITÉ - École Centrale de Nantes (Nantes Univ - ECN)
%+ Interaction et collaboration fluides à travers le continuum réalité-virtualité (SEAMLESS)
%A Bitar, Majd
%A Rivière, Jean-Philippe
%A Fribourg, Rebecca
%A Normand, Jean-Marie
%A Prié, Yannick
%< avec comité de lecture
%B LocXR 2025 - 3rd Workshop on Locomotion and Wayfinding in XR
%C Saint-Malo, France
%P 1-7
%8 2025-03-08
%D 2025
%K Virtual Reality
%K Locomotion
%K Social VR
%K Navigation
%K Collaborative Walking
%K Social Presence
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR]Conference papers
%X Walking together is a basic social interaction, making it an essential feature to support in social virtual reality. Most research on locomotion in VR focuses on the motor aspect of walking, rather than on the cognitive or social aspects of social walking. In this paper, we explore walking in pairs, draw on observations from a pilot study, and propose some recommendations for {enhancing the experience of} pair walking in social VR. {Using arm arm-swinging locomotion}, we implemented two features based on movement rhythm, a footstep cue and an aura to enhance the awareness of one's partner walking, that we tested in a custom virtual environment. The initial results highlight the potential benefits of our proposals, yet future work is needed to strengthen these findings.

title_s: Sustaining the Experience of Pair Natural Walking in Social Virtual Reality
abstract_s: Walking together is a basic social interaction, making it an essential feature to support in social virtual reality. Most research on locomotion in VR focuses on the motor aspect of walking, rather than on the cognitive or social aspects of social walking. In this paper, we explore walking in pairs, draw on observations from a pilot study, and propose some recommendations for {enhancing the experience of} pair walking in social VR. {Using arm arm-swinging locomotion}, we implemented two features based on movement rhythm, a footstep cue and an aura to enhance the awareness of one's partner walking, that we tested in a custom virtual environment. The initial results highlight the potential benefits of our proposals, yet future work is needed to strengthen these findings.
en_abstract_s: Walking together is a basic social interaction, making it an essential feature to support in social virtual reality. Most research on locomotion in VR focuses on the motor aspect of walking, rather than on the cognitive or social aspects of social walking. In this paper, we explore walking in pairs, draw on observations from a pilot study, and propose some recommendations for {enhancing the experience of} pair walking in social VR. {Using arm arm-swinging locomotion}, we implemented two features based on movement rhythm, a footstep cue and an aura to enhance the awareness of one's partner walking, that we tested in a custom virtual environment. The initial results highlight the potential benefits of our proposals, yet future work is needed to strengthen these findings.

Mathématiques - Numérique Nantes Université Nantes Université PEPR eNSEMBLE International No No Yes No Sustaining the Experience of Pair Natural Walking in Social Virtual Reality Majd Bitar 649785469edbd2e685e8d5f1698b1e6f etu.univ-nantes.fr 1484567 3357029-1484567 Jean-Philippe Rivière a8f3c107dfff175682667c1d4520bb02 gmail.com jean-phi-riviere 1328317 41131-1328317 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3529-1034 https://www.idref.fr/252740769 Rebecca Fribourg 00e434674682ec630de52b0a0cad3505 ec-nantes.fr rebecca-fribourg 1287108 1375646-1287108 https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=OE_oCioAAAAJ https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4572-477X https://www.idref.fr/25457923X Jean-Marie Normand dce532a07e06f5cee8c68eb1a0ee5697 ec-nantes.fr jean-marie-normand 13303 29068-13303 https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0557-4356 https://www.idref.fr/135414490 Yannick Prié 5f4da1ce0315e9bce83a6b92c5121484 univ-nantes.fr yannick-prie 1209 5699-1209 https://www.idref.fr/083348506 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7068-0836 https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=oZkePtQAAAAJ LocXR 2025 - 3rd Workshop on Locomotion and Wayfinding in XR 2025-03-08 2025-03-12 Saint-Malo France 1-7 2025 English Virtual Reality Locomotion Social VR Navigation Collaborative Walking Social Presence Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Walking together is a basic social interaction, making it an essential feature to support in social virtual reality. Most research on locomotion in VR focuses on the motor aspect of walking, rather than on the cognitive or social aspects of social walking. In this paper, we explore walking in pairs, draw on observations from a pilot study, and propose some recommendations for {enhancing the experience of} pair walking in social VR. {Using arm arm-swinging locomotion}, we implemented two features based on movement rhythm, a footstep cue and an aura to enhance the awareness of one's partner walking, that we tested in a custom virtual environment. The initial results highlight the potential benefits of our proposals, yet future work is needed to strengthen these findings.

%0 Conference Proceedings
%T Impact of Multimodal Instructions for Tool Manipulation Skills on Performance and User Experience in an Immersive Environment
%+ Informatique, BioInformatique, Systèmes Complexes (IBISC)
%A Simon, Cassandre
%A Boukli Hacene, Manel
%A Lebrun, Flavien
%A Otmane, Samir
%A Chellali, Amine
%< avec comité de lecture
%B 31st IEEE Conference On Virtual Reality And 3d User Interfaces (VR 2024)
%C Orlando, FL, United States
%P 670--680
%8 2024-03-16
%D 2024
%R 10.1109/VR58804.2024.00087
%K Immersive learning
%K Remote collaboration
%K Mentorship
%K Multimodal interactions
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Conference papers
%X With the mentoring model, a mentee can learn technical skills under the supervision of more experienced peers who demonstrate their knowledge through several communication modalities. Supporting the mentoring model within shared immersive training simulators holds promise in enhancing mentor-mentee interactions and learning outcomes in a safe environment. However, efficient communication within these spaces remains an open issue. This work presents a user study that explores the combination of communication modalities (verbal-visual, verbal-haptic, visual-haptic, and verbal-visual-haptic) to convey instructions to learners on the amplitude of movements to perform during a tool-handling task in an immersive environment. The study aims to examine the impact of the four modality combinations on performance (speed and accuracy of movement replication), mental workload, and participants’ user experience. The results show that participants achieved higher accuracy with the visual-haptic and verbal-visual-haptic conditions. Moreover, they performed the movements faster, and their movement trajectories were closer to the reference trajectories in the visual-haptic condition. Finally, the most preferred verbal-visual-haptic combination enhanced the users’ sense of presence, co-presence, social presence, modalities is the best suited for enhancing learners’ performance. Adding the verbal modality can also improve the user experience in the immersive learning environment. These findings contribute to improving the design of immersive collaborative systems and pave the way for exploring novel avenues of research into the efficacy of multimodal communication for enhancing the mentoring-based acquisition of technical skills in VR. These tools hold promise for diverse applications, including medical simulation.

%0 Conference Proceedings
%T A collaborative learning plateform combining co-embodiement and kinesthetic co-manipulation for technical Skill Acquisition in Virtual Reality
%T Une plateforme d'apprentissage collaboratif combinant le co-embodiment et la co-manipulation kinesthésique pour l'acquisition de comptétences techniques en réalité virtuelle
%+ Informatique, BioInformatique, Systèmes Complexes (IBISC)
%+ Institut des Systèmes Intelligents et de Robotique (ISIR)
%A Lebrun, Flavien
%A Saint-Bauzel, Ludovic
%A Chellali, Amine
%< avec comité de lecture
%Z Demo
%B IHM'25 - 36e Conférence Internationale Francophone sur l'Interaction Humain-Machine
%C Toulouse, France
%8 2025-11-03
%D 2025
%K Co-manipulation
%K Co-embodiment
%K Collaborative Learning
%K Technical Skill Learning
%K Virtual Reality
%K Co-manipulation
%K Co-incarnation
%K Apprentissage collaboratif
%K Apprentissage de Gestes Techniques
%K Réalité Virtuelle
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Conference papers
%X We propose a demonstration of a virtual reality platform for learning technical gestures. With this platform, an expert can guide a learner during their training. The guidance is visual through the co-embodiment of a single virtual avatar shared between the two users and also a kinesthetic co-manipulation link thanks to two haptic interfaces. The system will be used to explore the impact of the connection between the learner and the teacher on the long-term retention of a technical skill.
%X Nous proposons la démonstration d’une plateforme d’apprentissage de gestes techniques en réalité virtuelle. Avec cette plateforme, un expert peut guider un apprenant dans lors de son entrainement. Le guidage est visuel au travers de la co-incarnation d’un seul avatar virtuel partagé entre les deux utilisateurs et également kinesthésique grâce à deux interfaces à retour d’effort. Le dispositif servira à explorer l’impact du lien entre apprenant et expert pour la rétention à long terme d’un geste technique.

en_title_s: A collaborative learning plateform combining co-embodiement and kinesthetic co-manipulation for technical Skill Acquisition in Virtual Reality
fr_title_s: Une plateforme d'apprentissage collaboratif combinant le co-embodiment et la co-manipulation kinesthésique pour l'acquisition de comptétences techniques en réalité virtuelle
title_s: A collaborative learning plateform combining co-embodiement and kinesthetic co-manipulation for technical Skill Acquisition in Virtual Reality
Une plateforme d'apprentissage collaboratif combinant le co-embodiment et la co-manipulation kinesthésique pour l'acquisition de comptétences techniques en réalité virtuelle
abstract_s: We propose a demonstration of a virtual reality platform for learning technical gestures. With this platform, an expert can guide a learner during their training. The guidance is visual through the co-embodiment of a single virtual avatar shared between the two users and also a kinesthetic co-manipulation link thanks to two haptic interfaces. The system will be used to explore the impact of the connection between the learner and the teacher on the long-term retention of a technical skill.

Nous proposons la démonstration d’une plateforme d’apprentissage de gestes techniques en réalité virtuelle. Avec cette plateforme, un expert peut guider un apprenant dans lors de son entrainement. Le guidage est visuel au travers de la co-incarnation d’un seul avatar virtuel partagé entre les deux utilisateurs et également kinesthésique grâce à deux interfaces à retour d’effort. Le dispositif servira à explorer l’impact du lien entre apprenant et expert pour la rétention à long terme d’un geste technique.
en_abstract_s: We propose a demonstration of a virtual reality platform for learning technical gestures. With this platform, an expert can guide a learner during their training. The guidance is visual through the co-embodiment of a single virtual avatar shared between the two users and also a kinesthetic co-manipulation link thanks to two haptic interfaces. The system will be used to explore the impact of the connection between the learner and the teacher on the long-term retention of a technical skill.
fr_abstract_s: Nous proposons la démonstration d’une plateforme d’apprentissage de gestes techniques en réalité virtuelle. Avec cette plateforme, un expert peut guider un apprenant dans lors de son entrainement. Le guidage est visuel au travers de la co-incarnation d’un seul avatar virtuel partagé entre les deux utilisateurs et également kinesthésique grâce à deux interfaces à retour d’effort. Le dispositif servira à explorer l’impact du lien entre apprenant et expert pour la rétention à long terme d’un geste technique.

36e Conférence Internationale Francophone sur l'Interaction Humain-Machine 2025-11-03 2025-11-07 Toulouse France English Co-manipulation Co-embodiment Collaborative Learning Technical Skill Learning Virtual Reality Co-manipulation Co-incarnation Apprentissage collaboratif Apprentissage de Gestes Techniques Réalité Virtuelle Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

We propose a demonstration of a virtual reality platform for learning technical gestures. With this platform, an expert can guide a learner during their training. The guidance is visual through the co-embodiment of a single virtual avatar shared between the two users and also a kinesthetic co-manipulation link thanks to two haptic interfaces. The system will be used to explore the impact of the connection between the learner and the teacher on the long-term retention of a technical skill.

Nous proposons la démonstration d’une plateforme d’apprentissage de gestes techniques en réalité virtuelle. Avec cette plateforme, un expert peut guider un apprenant dans lors de son entrainement. Le guidage est visuel au travers de la co-incarnation d’un seul avatar virtuel partagé entre les deux utilisateurs et également kinesthésique grâce à deux interfaces à retour d’effort. Le dispositif servira à explorer l’impact du lien entre apprenant et expert pour la rétention à long terme d’un geste technique.

%X This position paper presents our ongoing PhD work exploring the integration of interpersonal haptic interactions into immersive VR training systems for first responders. The research focuses on enhancing user engagement, emotional resilience, and decision-making in high-stress scenarios by leveraging advanced haptic technologies, such as vibrotactile feedback and electromuscular stimulation. The primary goal is to model and simulate haptic interactions, such as touch sensations perceived by individuals in the real world, enhancing the realism of virtual training. This paper details the research objectives and the ongoing work and highlights the critical gaps this work aims to address in thecurrent VR training landscape.

université nantes angers le mans Interactions, Réalité Augmentée, Robotique Ambiante Laboratoire De Mécanique et d'Energétique d'Evry, EA 3332 Université d'Evry Val d'Essonne Laboratoire des Sciences du Numérique de Nantes Université Paris-Saclay UNIV-EVRY-SACLAY composantes instituts telecom Université Paris-Saclay ANR Graduate School Mathématiques Graduate School Computer Science Graduate School Life Sciences and Health Graduate School Sport, Mouvement, Facteurs Humains Nantes Université Nantes Université PEPR eNSEMBLE Maîtrise de l’Autre et de Soi sous Tension Emotionnelle, Restrictions de ressources et Stress – Masters Maîtrise de l’Autre et de Soi sous Tension Emotionnelle, Restrictions de ressources et Stress – Masters Maîtrise de l’Autre et de Soi sous Tension Emotionnelle, Restrictions de ressources et Stress – Masters International No No Yes Yes Bridging the Gap Between Real and Virtual Touch : Multisensory Avatars for Enhanced Training in Virtual Reality Manel Boukli Hacene a6cbdeae863b504aec93f421202125fd hotmail.com manel-boukli-hacene 1411728 2922701-1411728 https://orcid.org/0009-0003-0014-5111 https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=4lKdYcwAAAAJ 32nd IEEE Conference On Virtual Reality And 3d User Interfaces (VR 2025) 2025-03-08 2025-03-12 Saint-Malo France conferenceOrganizer IEEE Computer society Visualization and Graphics Technical Committee 2025-03-08 https://ieeevr.org/2025/program/posters/ English Haptic feedback Multisensory interaction Interpersonnal touch Human factors Professional training. Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

This position paper presents our ongoing PhD work exploring the integration of interpersonal haptic interactions into immersive VR training systems for first responders. The research focuses on enhancing user engagement, emotional resilience, and decision-making in high-stress scenarios by leveraging advanced haptic technologies, such as vibrotactile feedback and electromuscular stimulation. The primary goal is to model and simulate haptic interactions, such as touch sensations perceived by individuals in the real world, enhancing the realism of virtual training. This paper details the research objectives and the ongoing work and highlights the critical gaps this work aims to address in thecurrent VR training landscape.

%( 2025 IEEE International Symposium on Mixed and Augmented Reality (ISMAR)
%B ISMAR 2025 - IEEE International Symposium on Mixed and Augmented Reality
%C Daejon, South Korea
%P 717-727
%8 2025-10-08
%D 2025
%R 10.1109/ISMAR67309.2025.00081
%K Collaborative Problem-solving (CPS)
%K Interaction
%K Workspace
%K Learning
%K Collaboration
%K Virtual Reality (VR)
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Conference papers
%X Most work on collaborative immersive systems mimics real-world settings, using fully shared virtual workspaces that foster close collaboration. However, recent work in educational contexts using remote desktop environments suggests these shared approaches may not be optimal for learning, as it showed that individual workspaces lead to better learning outcomes. In this paper, we investigate whether individual workspaces also lead to better outcomes in a collaborative VR learning environment. We compare three distinct workspace configurations in a problem-solving task: (1) a fully shared environment where two users work on the same materials, (2) a replicated environment where each user has their own copy of the materials but can still see their partner and their workspace, and (3) a separated environment where users cannot see each other nor each other's workspace and each has individual materials. We evaluate how these configurations influenced collaborative interaction, problem-solving strategies, and learning. Our results suggest the replicated workspace reduced social experience and did not improve learning outcomes compared to the shared one, however, it allowed broader exploration of the problem space.

en_title_s: Shared, Replicated, or Separated? A Comparative Study of Virtual Workspace Configurations for Collaborative Hands-On Learning
title_s: Shared, Replicated, or Separated? A Comparative Study of Virtual Workspace Configurations for Collaborative Hands-On Learning
abstract_s: Most work on collaborative immersive systems mimics real-world settings, using fully shared virtual workspaces that foster close collaboration. However, recent work in educational contexts using remote desktop environments suggests these shared approaches may not be optimal for learning, as it showed that individual workspaces lead to better learning outcomes. In this paper, we investigate whether individual workspaces also lead to better outcomes in a collaborative VR learning environment. We compare three distinct workspace configurations in a problem-solving task: (1) a fully shared environment where two users work on the same materials, (2) a replicated environment where each user has their own copy of the materials but can still see their partner and their workspace, and (3) a separated environment where users cannot see each other nor each other's workspace and each has individual materials. We evaluate how these configurations influenced collaborative interaction, problem-solving strategies, and learning. Our results suggest the replicated workspace reduced social experience and did not improve learning outcomes compared to the shared one, however, it allowed broader exploration of the problem space.
en_abstract_s: Most work on collaborative immersive systems mimics real-world settings, using fully shared virtual workspaces that foster close collaboration. However, recent work in educational contexts using remote desktop environments suggests these shared approaches may not be optimal for learning, as it showed that individual workspaces lead to better learning outcomes. In this paper, we investigate whether individual workspaces also lead to better outcomes in a collaborative VR learning environment. We compare three distinct workspace configurations in a problem-solving task: (1) a fully shared environment where two users work on the same materials, (2) a replicated environment where each user has their own copy of the materials but can still see their partner and their workspace, and (3) a separated environment where users cannot see each other nor each other's workspace and each has individual materials. We evaluate how these configurations influenced collaborative interaction, problem-solving strategies, and learning. Our results suggest the replicated workspace reduced social experience and did not improve learning outcomes compared to the shared one, however, it allowed broader exploration of the problem space.

A Comparative Study of Virtual Workspace Configurations for Collaborative Hands-On Learning Juliette Le Meudec b2c0bbb54d7728923257ff83038c7aca inria.fr juliette-le-meudec 1535702 3074334-1535702 Anastasia Bezerianos 8a70f1ba232a34aa6f7c79a0dc9299b3 universite-paris-saclay.fr anastasia-bezerianos 16697 24840-16697 https://www.idref.fr/22012065X https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7142-2548 https://viaf.org/viaf/146151302936548660470 Arnaud Prouzeau a4ee01dc9b4d4f6b45290cb15bd12e3b lri.fr arnaud-prouzeau 5835 27387-5835 https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3800-5870 https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=rEf29d0AAAAJ https://www.idref.fr/223383864 https://viaf.org/viaf/68151836609020402602 2025 IEEE International Symposium on Mixed and Augmented Reality (ISMAR) ISMAR 2025 - IEEE International Symposium on Mixed and Augmented Reality 2025-10-08 2025-10-12 Daejon South Korea 717-727 10.1109/ISMAR67309.2025.00081 English Collaborative Problem-solving (CPS) Interaction Workspace Learning Collaboration Virtual Reality (VR) Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Most work on collaborative immersive systems mimics real-world settings, using fully shared virtual workspaces that foster close collaboration. However, recent work in educational contexts using remote desktop environments suggests these shared approaches may not be optimal for learning, as it showed that individual workspaces lead to better learning outcomes. In this paper, we investigate whether individual workspaces also lead to better outcomes in a collaborative VR learning environment. We compare three distinct workspace configurations in a problem-solving task: (1) a fully shared environment where two users work on the same materials, (2) a replicated environment where each user has their own copy of the materials but can still see their partner and their workspace, and (3) a separated environment where users cannot see each other nor each other's workspace and each has individual materials. We evaluate how these configurations influenced collaborative interaction, problem-solving strategies, and learning. Our results suggest the replicated workspace reduced social experience and did not improve learning outcomes compared to the shared one, however, it allowed broader exploration of the problem space.

%X Mixed Reality (MR) is increasingly being studied for synchronous remote collaboration in various fields, from industrial maintenance to education. It allows to combine virtual reality, augmented reality, and 2D interfaces to visualize and share real and virtual content. The notion of coupling of collaborative activities, central to studies of Computer-Supported Cooperative Work (CSCW) applications, takes various forms with MR due to its 3D and immersive characteristics, as well as the heterogeneity of interactive technologies. To cope with this diversity, we propose a graphical notation, PV*, that precisely describes the coupling relationships between multiple viewpoints on physical or virtual views, as well as their control by collaborators. We study the descriptive power of PV* by describing several collaborative situations from the literature and discuss its generative power by exploring several design possibilities described with PV*.
%X La Réalité Mixte (RM) est de plus en plus étudiée pour collaborer à distance de façon synchrone dans de nombreux domaines, de la maintenance industrielle à l’éducation. Elle permet de combiner des technologies de réalité virtuelle, de réalité augmentée, ainsi que des interfaces 2D, pour visualiser et partager du contenu réel et virtuel. La notion de couplage de l’activité, centrale aux études des collecticiels, prend des formes variées avec la RM du fait de ses caractéristiques 3D et immersives, ainsi que de l’hétérogénéité des technologies interactives. Face à cette diversité, nous proposons une notation graphique, PV*, décrivant précisément les relations de couplage entre les points de vue sur des vues physiques ou virtuelles, ainsi que leur contrôle par les personnes collaborant. Nous étudions le pouvoir descriptif de PV* en décrivant plusieurs situations collaboratives de la littérature et illustrons son pouvoir génératif en explorant plusieurs alternatives de conception décrites avec PV*.

36e Conférence Internationale Francophone sur l'Interaction Humain-Machine 2025-11-04 2025-11-07 Toulouse France conferenceOrganizer AFIHM Université de Toulouse ENAC IHM’25 : Articles Scientifiques de la 36ème conférence Francophone sur l’Interaction Humain-Machine 10.1145/3765712.3765723 French Control. Coupling Point of View Collaboration Mixed Reality contrôle couplage point de vue collaboration Réalité mixte Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Mixed Reality (MR) is increasingly being studied for synchronous remote collaboration in various fields, from industrial maintenance to education. It allows to combine virtual reality, augmented reality, and 2D interfaces to visualize and share real and virtual content. The notion of coupling of collaborative activities, central to studies of Computer-Supported Cooperative Work (CSCW) applications, takes various forms with MR due to its 3D and immersive characteristics, as well as the heterogeneity of interactive technologies. To cope with this diversity, we propose a graphical notation, PV*, that precisely describes the coupling relationships between multiple viewpoints on physical or virtual views, as well as their control by collaborators. We study the descriptive power of PV* by describing several collaborative situations from the literature and discuss its generative power by exploring several design possibilities described with PV*.

La Réalité Mixte (RM) est de plus en plus étudiée pour collaborer à distance de façon synchrone dans de nombreux domaines, de la maintenance industrielle à l’éducation. Elle permet de combiner des technologies de réalité virtuelle, de réalité augmentée, ainsi que des interfaces 2D, pour visualiser et partager du contenu réel et virtuel. La notion de couplage de l’activité, centrale aux études des collecticiels, prend des formes variées avec la RM du fait de ses caractéristiques 3D et immersives, ainsi que de l’hétérogénéité des technologies interactives. Face à cette diversité, nous proposons une notation graphique, PV*, décrivant précisément les relations de couplage entre les points de vue sur des vues physiques ou virtuelles, ainsi que leur contrôle par les personnes collaborant. Nous étudions le pouvoir descriptif de PV* en décrivant plusieurs situations collaboratives de la littérature et illustrons son pouvoir génératif en explorant plusieurs alternatives de conception décrites avec PV*.

%0 Unpublished work
%T Maintaining Physical Collaboration with Haptic Feedback in Virtual Reality
%+ Sorbonne Université (SU)
%+ Institut des Systèmes Intelligents et de Robotique (ISIR)
%A Tseng, Wen-Jie
%8 2026-03-16
%D 2026
%K remote collaboration
%K physical interaction
%K haptic feedback
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Preprints, Working Papers, ...
%X While Virtual Reality (VR) can benefit remote and synchronous collaboration, it is still limited to audiovisual experiences. Vibrotactile feedback provided by VR controllers is feasible; however, it cannot represent force feedback. Moreover, each user may have a different physical configuration and haptic interface. In this position paper, I aim to design an intelligent system that can maintain physical collaboration with haptic feedback in VR. This approach is challenging because the system must consider many properties from both physical spaces and the shared virtual environment, delivering the haptic solution in real-time. I will discuss the challenges of maintaining physical collaboration in VR and how AI can play a role in adapting haptic solutions.

%0 Conference Proceedings
%T A Systematic Literature Review to Characterize Asymmetric Interaction in Collaborative Systems
%T Une Revue Systématique de la Littérature pour Caractériser l'Interaction Asymétrique dans les Systèmes Collaboratifs
%+ Etude de L’Interaction Personne SystèmE (IRIT-ELIPSE)
%+ Interacting with Large Data - LISN (ILDA)
%A Bréhault, Victor
%A Dubois, Emmanuel
%A Prouzeau, Arnaud
%A Serrano, Marcos
%< avec comité de lecture
%( Proceedings of the 2025 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
%B CHI 2025 - CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
%C Yokohama, Japan
%8 2025-04-26
%D 2025
%R 10.1145/3706598.3713129
%K Remote collaboration
%K asymmetric interaction
%K systematic literature review
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Conference papers
%X Computer-mediated collaboration often relies on symmetrical interactions between users, where all the collaborators use identical devices. However, in some cases, either due to constraints (e.g. users in different environments) or by choice (e.g. using devices with different properties), users engage in asymmetrical interactions. Addressing such asymmetries in heterogeneous systems can be difficult as there has been no systematic analysis of how to define them, or their impact on collaboration. In this paper, we characterize the asymmetries that can arise between users' interactions within collaborative heterogeneous systems. To this end, we conduct a systematic literature review of asymmetric collaborative systems, coding their properties, including the interaction spaces, their input and output modalities, and shared feedback. We then define the dimensions of asymmetry that emerge from this review. We discuss their impact on collaboration and outline a set of challenges and opportunities for future research.
%X La collaboration assistée par ordinateur repose souvent sur des interactions symétriques entre les utilisateurs, où tous les collaborateurs utilisent des appareils identiques. Cependant, dans certains cas, soit en raison de contraintes (par exemple, des utilisateurs dans des environnements différents), soit par choix (par exemple, l'utilisation d'appareils ayant des propriétés différentes), les utilisateurs s'engagent dans des interactions asymétriques. Il peut être difficile de traiter ces asymétries dans les systèmes hétérogènes, car il n'y a pas eu d'analyse systématique de la manière de les définir ou de leur impact sur la collaboration. Dans cet article, nous caractérisons les asymétries qui peuvent survenir entre les interactions des utilisateurs dans les systèmes hétérogènes collaboratifs. À cette fin, nous procédons à une analyse systématique de la littérature sur les systèmes collaboratifs asymétriques, en codant leurs propriétés, y compris les espaces d'interaction, leurs modalités d'entrée et de sortie, et le retour d'information partagé. Nous définissons ensuite les dimensions de l'asymétrie qui ressortent de cette étude. Pour finir, nous discutons de leur impact sur la collaboration et soulignons un ensemble de défis et d'opportunités pour la recherche future.

title_s: A Systematic Literature Review to Characterize Asymmetric Interaction in Collaborative Systems

abstract_s: Computer-mediated collaboration often relies on symmetrical interactions between users, where all the collaborators use identical devices. However, in some cases, either due to constraints (e.g. users in different environments) or by choice (e.g. using devices with different properties), users engage in asymmetrical interactions. Addressing such asymmetries in heterogeneous systems can be difficult as there has been no systematic analysis of how to define them, or their impact on collaboration. In this paper, we characterize the asymmetries that can arise between users' interactions within collaborative heterogeneous systems. To this end, we conduct a systematic literature review of asymmetric collaborative systems, coding their properties, including the interaction spaces, their input and output modalities, and shared feedback. We then define the dimensions of asymmetry that emerge from this review. We discuss their impact on collaboration and outline a set of challenges and opportunities for future research.

La collaboration assistée par ordinateur repose souvent sur des interactions symétriques entre les utilisateurs, où tous les collaborateurs utilisent des appareils identiques. Cependant, dans certains cas, soit en raison de contraintes (par exemple, des utilisateurs dans des environnements différents), soit par choix (par exemple, l'utilisation d'appareils ayant des propriétés différentes), les utilisateurs s'engagent dans des interactions asymétriques. Il peut être difficile de traiter ces asymétries dans les systèmes hétérogènes, car il n'y a pas eu d'analyse systématique de la manière de les définir ou de leur impact sur la collaboration. Dans cet article, nous caractérisons les asymétries qui peuvent survenir entre les interactions des utilisateurs dans les systèmes hétérogènes collaboratifs. À cette fin, nous procédons à une analyse systématique de la littérature sur les systèmes collaboratifs asymétriques, en codant leurs propriétés, y compris les espaces d'interaction, leurs modalités d'entrée et de sortie, et le retour d'information partagé. Nous définissons ensuite les dimensions de l'asymétrie qui ressortent de cette étude. Pour finir, nous discutons de leur impact sur la collaboration et soulignons un ensemble de défis et d'opportunités pour la recherche future.

CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems 2025-04-26 2025-05-01 Yokohama Japan conferenceOrganizer ACM 2025-04-26 10.1145/3706598.3713129 English Remote collaboration asymmetric interaction systematic literature review Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Computer-mediated collaboration often relies on symmetrical interactions between users, where all the collaborators use identical devices. However, in some cases, either due to constraints (e.g. users in different environments) or by choice (e.g. using devices with different properties), users engage in asymmetrical interactions. Addressing such asymmetries in heterogeneous systems can be difficult as there has been no systematic analysis of how to define them, or their impact on collaboration. In this paper, we characterize the asymmetries that can arise between users' interactions within collaborative heterogeneous systems. To this end, we conduct a systematic literature review of asymmetric collaborative systems, coding their properties, including the interaction spaces, their input and output modalities, and shared feedback. We then define the dimensions of asymmetry that emerge from this review. We discuss their impact on collaboration and outline a set of challenges and opportunities for future research.

La collaboration assistée par ordinateur repose souvent sur des interactions symétriques entre les utilisateurs, où tous les collaborateurs utilisent des appareils identiques. Cependant, dans certains cas, soit en raison de contraintes (par exemple, des utilisateurs dans des environnements différents), soit par choix (par exemple, l'utilisation d'appareils ayant des propriétés différentes), les utilisateurs s'engagent dans des interactions asymétriques. Il peut être difficile de traiter ces asymétries dans les systèmes hétérogènes, car il n'y a pas eu d'analyse systématique de la manière de les définir ou de leur impact sur la collaboration. Dans cet article, nous caractérisons les asymétries qui peuvent survenir entre les interactions des utilisateurs dans les systèmes hétérogènes collaboratifs. À cette fin, nous procédons à une analyse systématique de la littérature sur les systèmes collaboratifs asymétriques, en codant leurs propriétés, y compris les espaces d'interaction, leurs modalités d'entrée et de sortie, et le retour d'information partagé. Nous définissons ensuite les dimensions de l'asymétrie qui ressortent de cette étude. Pour finir, nous discutons de leur impact sur la collaboration et soulignons un ensemble de défis et d'opportunités pour la recherche future.

%0 Journal Article
%T “Persuasive Vibrations”: Studying the influence of vibration parameters on speech persuasion
%+ Interaction et collaboration fluides à travers le continuum réalité-virtualité (SEAMLESS)
%A Toofany, Sabrina
%A Lécuyer, Anatole
%A Argelaguet, Ferran
%A Saint-Aubert, Justine
%< avec comité de lecture
%@ 1939-1412
%J IEEE Transactions on Haptics (ToH)
%I IEEE
%P 1 - 12
%8 2025
%D 2025
%R 10.1109/toh.2025.3600579
%K Speech
%K Social Communication
%K Persuasion
%K Vibrotactile feedback
%K Haptic
%K Audio
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Journal articles
%X This paper investigates the notion of "Persuasive Vibrations", which showed that augmenting a person's speech with vibrotactile feedback could artificially increase persuasion. However, while the initial paper has shown the effect, the underlying reasons why vibrations enhance persuasion remain unknown. Through two different user studies, this paper aims to study how the underlying parameters of the vibratory feedback (e.g., frequency, amplitude, or audio-vibration synchronization) influence persuasion. The first study aimed to identify the parameters of vibrotactile feedback that can positively influence persuasion. The second study evaluated vibrotactile feedback that might impair the persuasive effect. In a nutshell, the first experiment suggests that the isolation of different properties of the vibratory signal could tend to provide higher persuasion compared to no vibratory feedback. A lower frequency at 100 Hz seems the most efficient way to generate a persuasive effect. In contrast, the second experiment suggests that some alteration of the vibratory signal ( e.g., latency) does not decrease the levels of persuasion compared to the no-vibration condition. All in all, the results suggest that using lower frequencies could have a better effect on persuasion. These results could serve as a basis for haptic design in applications like videoconferencing, virtual meetings, and training systems where supporting user speech is essential.
%G English

12 2025 10.1109/toh.2025.3600579 English Speech Social Communication Persuasion Vibrotactile feedback Haptic Audio Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Journal articles Journal articles Journal articles

This paper investigates the notion of "Persuasive Vibrations", which showed that augmenting a person's speech with vibrotactile feedback could artificially increase persuasion. However, while the initial paper has shown the effect, the underlying reasons why vibrations enhance persuasion remain unknown. Through two different user studies, this paper aims to study how the underlying parameters of the vibratory feedback (e.g., frequency, amplitude, or audio-vibration synchronization) influence persuasion. The first study aimed to identify the parameters of vibrotactile feedback that can positively influence persuasion. The second study evaluated vibrotactile feedback that might impair the persuasive effect. In a nutshell, the first experiment suggests that the isolation of different properties of the vibratory signal could tend to provide higher persuasion compared to no vibratory feedback. A lower frequency at 100 Hz seems the most efficient way to generate a persuasive effect. In contrast, the second experiment suggests that some alteration of the vibratory signal ( e.g., latency) does not decrease the levels of persuasion compared to the no-vibration condition. All in all, the results suggest that using lower frequencies could have a better effect on persuasion. These results could serve as a basis for haptic design in applications like videoconferencing, virtual meetings, and training systems where supporting user speech is essential.

%X Extended Reality (XR) technologies are increasingly proposed to support Deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) users, yet there remains limited understanding of how these approaches align with Deaf community perspectives. Through a critical review of 26 XR research papers involving DHH participants, we identify three recurring issues: sound-first framings that treat access to audio as universally desirable, misrepresentations of sign language that overlook its spatial-visual structure, and hearing-led methodological practices that conflate Deaf and deaf/hard of hearing users into a single category. We argue that these patterns limit the relevance and impact of XR for Deaf communities and reinforce long-standing forms of audism in design. We propose a Deaf-centered reframing of XR accessibility and offer recommendations for both hearing and Deaf researchers to support culturally grounded, linguistically informed, and community-led XR design.

abstract_s: Extended Reality (XR) technologies are increasingly proposed to support Deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) users, yet there remains limited understanding of how these approaches align with Deaf community perspectives. Through a critical review of 26 XR research papers involving DHH participants, we identify three recurring issues: sound-first framings that treat access to audio as universally desirable, misrepresentations of sign language that overlook its spatial-visual structure, and hearing-led methodological practices that conflate Deaf and deaf/hard of hearing users into a single category. We argue that these patterns limit the relevance and impact of XR for Deaf communities and reinforce long-standing forms of audism in design. We propose a Deaf-centered reframing of XR accessibility and offer recommendations for both hearing and Deaf researchers to support culturally grounded, linguistically informed, and community-led XR design.
en_abstract_s: Extended Reality (XR) technologies are increasingly proposed to support Deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) users, yet there remains limited understanding of how these approaches align with Deaf community perspectives. Through a critical review of 26 XR research papers involving DHH participants, we identify three recurring issues: sound-first framings that treat access to audio as universally desirable, misrepresentations of sign language that overlook its spatial-visual structure, and hearing-led methodological practices that conflate Deaf and deaf/hard of hearing users into a single category. We argue that these patterns limit the relevance and impact of XR for Deaf communities and reinforce long-standing forms of audism in design. We propose a Deaf-centered reframing of XR accessibility and offer recommendations for both hearing and Deaf researchers to support culturally grounded, linguistically informed, and community-led XR design.

A Critical Review of Extended Reality Research on Deaf and Hard of Hearing Users Deaf Tech Conference 2026 Shuxu Huffman 4129811-0 Michaela Okosi 4129812-0 Joe Merino 4129813-0 Paige Devries 4129814-0 Tifanie Bouchara cc9269becd02992dbd0a3c75e08a20b9 universite-paris-saclay.fr tifanie-bouchara 181706 581762-181706 https://arxiv.org/a/bouchara_t_1 https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2175-5384 https://www.idref.fr/166491977 http://isni.org/isni/0000000399645193 https://viaf.org/viaf/294898450 Christian Vogler 1736308-0 Abraham Glasser 4129815-0 Raja Kushalnagar 4129816-0 Tifanie BOUCHARA cc9269becd02992dbd0a3c75e08a20b9 universite-paris-saclay.fr National Institute on Disability, Independent Living, and Rehabilitation Research (NIDILRR grant number 90REGE0027) 2026-05-20 22:04:34 2026-05-27 14:07:01 2026-05-22 09:41:18 2026-05-26 2026-05-20 contributor Tifanie BOUCHARA cc9269becd02992dbd0a3c75e08a20b9 universite-paris-saclay.fr CCSD hal-05628514 https://hal.science/hal-05628514 huffman:hal-05628514 <i>1st International Deaf Tech Conference</i>, May 2026, Vienne, Austria. <a target="_blank" href="https://dx.doi.org/10.21942/uva.32090170.v6">⟨10.21942/uva.32090170.v6⟩</a> 1st International Deaf Tech Conference, May 2026, Vienne, Austria. ⟨10.21942/uva.32090170.v6⟩ CC BY-NC 4.0 - Attribution - Non-commercial use CNRS - Centre national de la recherche scientifique Ecole CentraleSupélec Université Paris-Saclay Université Paris-Saclay ANR Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire des Sciences du Numérique Graduate School Computer Science Virtual & augmented ENvIronments for Simulation & Experiments PEPR eNSEMBLE Continuité collaborative du numérique vers l'humain International No No Yes No XR for Whom? A Critical Review of Extended Reality Research on Deaf and Hard of Hearing Users Deaf Tech Conference 2026 Shuxu Huffman 4129811-0 Michaela Okosi 4129812-0 Joe Merino 4129813-0 Paige Devries 4129814-0 Tifanie Bouchara cc9269becd02992dbd0a3c75e08a20b9 universite-paris-saclay.fr tifanie-bouchara 181706 581762-181706 https://arxiv.org/a/bouchara_t_1 https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2175-5384 https://www.idref.fr/166491977 http://isni.org/isni/0000000399645193 https://viaf.org/viaf/294898450 Christian Vogler 1736308-0 Abraham Glasser 4129815-0 Raja Kushalnagar 4129816-0 1st International Deaf Tech Conference 2026-05-26 2026-05-27 Vienne Austria University of Amsterdam Amsterdam University of Applied Sciences 2026-05-26 10.21942/uva.32090170.v6 English Deaf Tech Extended Reality Communication and Culture Language Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Extended Reality (XR) technologies are increasingly proposed to support Deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) users, yet there remains limited understanding of how these approaches align with Deaf community perspectives. Through a critical review of 26 XR research papers involving DHH participants, we identify three recurring issues: sound-first framings that treat access to audio as universally desirable, misrepresentations of sign language that overlook its spatial-visual structure, and hearing-led methodological practices that conflate Deaf and deaf/hard of hearing users into a single category. We argue that these patterns limit the relevance and impact of XR for Deaf communities and reinforce long-standing forms of audism in design. We propose a Deaf-centered reframing of XR accessibility and offer recommendations for both hearing and Deaf researchers to support culturally grounded, linguistically informed, and community-led XR design.

%X Recent artistic explorations in VR environments have explored how users engage with virtual spaces, sounds, and bodies whether, as artists, coders, or spectators. While many applications focus primarily on musical interaction or motion capture, few allow users to actively compose spatial and temporal relationships within a multimodal collaborative creation context merging altogether different artistic modalities. In this project, we present a Virtual Reality application that enables novel forms of collaboration between dancers and musicians. The application allows dancers to record full-body performances, which musicians can then use as the basis for sonic composition by spatially mapping sound triggers onto the dancer’s recorded movement. Rather than relying on live capture or realtime gesture tracking, our approach treats movement as a timelinefor interaction blending choreography with sound design in an asynchronous workflow. We employ an iterative design processto ensure usability among experts. This paper details the first implementation and study involving 10 participants recruited from professional and amateur artists with electronic music backgrounds, highlighting positive reception of the application’s creative potential and usability.

en_title_s: Toward Multimodal Asynchronous Collaboration in VR Artistic Creation with SPARK
title_s: Toward Multimodal Asynchronous Collaboration in VR Artistic Creation with SPARK
abstract_s: Recent artistic explorations in VR environments have explored how users engage with virtual spaces, sounds, and bodies whether, as artists, coders, or spectators. While many applications focus primarily on musical interaction or motion capture, few allow users to actively compose spatial and temporal relationships within a multimodal collaborative creation context merging altogether different artistic modalities. In this project, we present a Virtual Reality application that enables novel forms of collaboration between dancers and musicians. The application allows dancers to record full-body performances, which musicians can then use as the basis for sonic composition by spatially mapping sound triggers onto the dancer’s recorded movement. Rather than relying on live capture or realtime gesture tracking, our approach treats movement as a timeline for interaction blending choreography with sound design in an asynchronous workflow. We employ an iterative design process to ensure usability among experts. This paper details the first implementation and study involving 10 participants recruited from professional and amateur artists with electronic music backgrounds, highlighting positive reception of the application’s creative potential and usability.
en_abstract_s: Recent artistic explorations in VR environments have explored how users engage with virtual spaces, sounds, and bodies whether, as artists, coders, or spectators. While many applications focus primarily on musical interaction or motion capture, few allow users to actively compose spatial and temporal relationships within a multimodal collaborative creation context merging altogether different artistic modalities. In this project, we present a Virtual Reality application that enables novel forms of collaboration between dancers and musicians. The application allows dancers to record full-body performances, which musicians can then use as the basis for sonic composition by spatially mapping sound triggers onto the dancer’s recorded movement. Rather than relying on live capture or realtime gesture tracking, our approach treats movement as a timeline for interaction blending choreography with sound design in an asynchronous workflow. We employ an iterative design process to ensure usability among experts. This paper details the first implementation and study involving 10 participants recruited from professional and amateur artists with electronic music backgrounds, highlighting positive reception of the application’s creative potential and usability.

Virtual Reality Software and Technology 2025-11-12 2025-11-14 Montréal Canada 1-10 2025 10.1145/3756884.3765998 English Auditory feedback Virtual reality Usability testing User studies Interaction design Human computer interaction (HCI) User-Centered Design Spatial Sound Composition Asynchronous Interaction Creative Collaboration Multimodal Interaction Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Recent artistic explorations in VR environments have explored how users engage with virtual spaces, sounds, and bodies whether, as artists, coders, or spectators. While many applications focus primarily on musical interaction or motion capture, few allow users to actively compose spatial and temporal relationships within a multimodal collaborative creation context merging altogether different artistic modalities. In this project, we present a Virtual Reality application that enables novel forms of collaboration between dancers and musicians. The application allows dancers to record full-body performances, which musicians can then use as the basis for sonic composition by spatially mapping sound triggers onto the dancer’s recorded movement. Rather than relying on live capture or realtime gesture tracking, our approach treats movement as a timelinefor interaction blending choreography with sound design in an asynchronous workflow. We employ an iterative design processto ensure usability among experts. This paper details the first implementation and study involving 10 participants recruited from professional and amateur artists with electronic music backgrounds, highlighting positive reception of the application’s creative potential and usability.

title_s: Simulation Immersive pour la Formation des forces de l'ordre : Enrichir la Gestion de Conflits Interpersonnels par l'Intégration du Toucher en Réalité Virtuelle
Immersive Simulation for Law Enforcement Training: Enhancing Interpersonal Conflict Management Through Touch Integration in Virtual Reality
abstract_s: L’intégration du retour haptique dans les simulateurs de réalité virtuelle ouvre de nouvelles perspectives pour la formation professionnelle, notamment dans les métiers impliquant la gestion de conflits interpersonnels. Cet article présente une approche immersive qui combine interactions sociales simulées et sensations haptiques, appliquée à un scénario réaliste d’intervention.

Integrating tactile feedback into virtual reality simulators offers new opportunities for professional training, especially in fields involving interpersonal conflict management. This article presents an immersive approach that combines simulated social interactions and haptic sensations, applied to a realistic intervention scenario.

Cet article présente une approche immersive qui combine interactions sociales simulées et sensations haptiques, appliquée à un scénario réaliste d’intervention.

Demo IHM’25 : Actes étendus de la 36ème conférence Francophone sur l’Interaction Humain-Machine IHM'25 - 36e Conférence Internationale Francophone sur l'Interaction Humain-Machine 2025-11-03 2025-11-07 Toulouse France French Professional training Human factors Interpersonal touch Multisensory interaction Haptic feedback Retour haptique formation professionnelle facteurs humains toucher interpersonnel interaction multisensorielle Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Integrating tactile feedback into virtual reality simulators offers new opportunities for professional training, especially in fields involving interpersonal conflict management. This article presents an immersive approach that combines simulated social interactions and haptic sensations, applied to a realistic intervention scenario.

L’intégration du retour haptique dans les simulateurs de réalité virtuelle ouvre de nouvelles perspectives pour la formation professionnelle, notamment dans les métiers impliquant la gestion de conflits interpersonnels. Cet article présente une approche immersive qui combine interactions sociales simulées et sensations haptiques, appliquée à un scénario réaliste d’intervention.

%0 Conference Proceedings
%T Influence of multimodal instructions on learning tool manipulation skills through mentoring in an immersive environment: Influence des instructions multimodales sur l’apprentissage par compagnonnage des compétences de manipulation d’outil dans un environnement immersif
%+ Informatique, BioInformatique, Systèmes Complexes (IBISC)
%A Simon, Cassandre
%A Hacene, Manel Boukli
%A Lebrun, Flavien
%A Otmane, Samir
%A Chellali, Amine
%< avec comité de lecture
%B 35th International Francophone Conference on Human-Computer Interaction (IHM 2024)
%C Paris, France
%I ACM
%P 1-13
%8 2024-03-25
%D 2024
%R 10.1145/3649792.3649793
%K Skill learning
%K Multimodal interactions
%K Mentorship
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Conference papers
%X Mentorship allows novices to acquire skills under the guidance of experts who use various communication modalities. However, reproducing this model in immersive simulators remains challenging, particularly in ensuring effective communication between experts and novices. Our study explores the impact of multimodal expertnovice communication to transmit instructions on the amplitude of movements in a tool manipulation task in an immersive environment. The results reveal that combining visual-haptic modalities improves movements precision, speed, and quality. Additionally, the verbal-visual-haptic combination enhances the sense of presence, co-presence, and learning experience. These results suggest that the visual-haptic combination is optimal for improving novice performance, and integrating the verbal modality enhances the user experience. These findings open new perspectives for improving the acquisition of technical skills through VR mentorship using multimodal communication.

Mentorship allows novices to acquire skills under the guidance of experts who use various communication modalities. However, reproducing this model in immersive simulators remains challenging, particularly in ensuring effective communication between experts and novices. Our study explores the impact of multimodal expertnovice communication to transmit instructions on the amplitude of movements in a tool manipulation task in an immersive environment. The results reveal that combining visual-haptic modalities improves movements precision, speed, and quality. Additionally, the verbal-visual-haptic combination enhances the sense of presence, co-presence, and learning experience. These results suggest that the visual-haptic combination is optimal for improving novice performance, and integrating the verbal modality enhances the user experience. These findings open new perspectives for improving the acquisition of technical skills through VR mentorship using multimodal communication.
