%0 Unpublished work
%T FlowC2S: Flowing from Current to Succeeding Frames for Fast and Memory-Efficient Video Continuation
%+ Augmented Reality & Artificial Intelligence - LISN (ARAI)
%+ Institut Polytechnique de Paris (IP Paris)
%+ Département Images, Données, Signal (IDS)
%+ Image, Modélisation, Analyse, GEométrie, Synthèse (IMAGES)
%+ Laboratoire Interdisciplinaire des Sciences du Numérique (LISN)
%A Margaryan, Hovhannes
%A Bammey, Quentin
%A Sandor, Christian
%8 2026-04-30
%D 2026
%Z 2604.17625v1
%K video continuation
%K flow matching
%K inherent optimal couplings
%K target inversion
%Z Computer Science [cs]Preprints, Working Papers, ...
%X This paper introduces a novel methodology for generating fast and memory-efficient video continuations. Our method, dubbed FlowC2S, fine-tunes a pre-trained text-to-video flow model to learn a vector field between the current and succeeding video chunks. Two design choices are key. First, we introduce inherent optimal couplings, utilizing temporally adjacent video chunks during training as a practical proxy for true optimal couplings, resulting in straighter flows. Second, we incorporate target inversion, injecting the inverted latent of the target chunk into the input representation to strengthen correspondences and improve visual fidelity. By flowing directly from current to succeeding frames, instead of the common combination of current frames with noise to generate a video continuation, we reduce the dimensionality of the model input by a factor of two. The proposed method, fine-tuned from LTXV and Wan, surpasses the state-of-the-art scores across quantitative evaluations with FID and FVD, with as few as five neural function evaluations.

This paper introduces a novel methodology for generating fast and memory-efficient video continuations. Our method, dubbed FlowC2S, fine-tunes a pre-trained text-to-video flow model to learn a vector field between the current and succeeding video chunks. Two design choices are key. First, we introduce inherent optimal couplings, utilizing temporally adjacent video chunks during training as a practical proxy for true optimal couplings, resulting in straighter flows. Second, we incorporate target inversion, injecting the inverted latent of the target chunk into the input representation to strengthen correspondences and improve visual fidelity. By flowing directly from current to succeeding frames, instead of the common combination of current frames with noise to generate a video continuation, we reduce the dimensionality of the model input by a factor of two. The proposed method, fine-tuned from LTXV and Wan, surpasses the state-of-the-art scores across quantitative evaluations with FID and FVD, with as few as five neural function evaluations.

%X Extended reality and motion capture technologies have beenexplored as tools for embodied artistic performance, allowingdancers and musicians to interact with sound within immersiveenvironments. However, motion capture systems primarily trackgross motor gestures and solid body segments, which restrictstheir ability to represent dance styles that rely on muscular activationand subtle movement rather than large gestures. Electromyography(EMG), by contrast, captures muscular activity and canreflect finer body movements and soft tissue deformation thatmay not produce large-scale motion, yet are widely used in danceto convey expressive sensibility. In this work, we present Kino-Groove, an extended reality interface that integrates EMG withmotion capture to support expressive mapping of dance to sound.KinoGroove was developed through discussions with dancersand artists, involving close collaboration for testing, feedback,and data acquisition. Through two usage scenarios,one focusing on large-amplitude movements and the other onlow-amplitude muscular activation, we illustrate how the systemenables artists to shape sound through both spatial displacementand muscular intensity. We finally describe methods for synchronizedacquisition, processing, and visualization of motion andEMG data.

en_title_s: KinoGroove: Composing with Muscle and Motion in Extended Reality
title_s: KinoGroove: Composing with Muscle and Motion in Extended Reality
en_keyword_s: Extended RealityXR Virtual Reality VR Multimodal Interaction Creative Collaboration Asynchronous Interaction Spatial Sound Composition Motion Capture Electromyography EMG
keyword_s: Extended RealityXR Virtual Reality VR Multimodal Interaction Creative Collaboration Asynchronous Interaction Spatial Sound Composition Motion Capture Electromyography EMG
abstract_s: Extended reality and motion capture technologies have been explored as tools for embodied artistic performance, allowing dancers and musicians to interact with sound within immersive environments. However, motion capture systems primarily track gross motor gestures and solid body segments, which restricts their ability to represent dance styles that rely on muscular activation and subtle movement rather than large gestures. Electromyography (EMG), by contrast, captures muscular activity and can reflect finer body movements and soft tissue deformation that may not produce large-scale motion, yet are widely used in dance to convey expressive sensibility. In this work, we present Kino- Groove, an extended reality interface that integrates EMG with motion capture to support expressive mapping of dance to sound. KinoGroove was developed through discussions with dancers and artists, involving close collaboration for testing, feedback, and data acquisition. Our system allows musicians to generate and control musical structures through both visible motion and internal effort, covering a wider range of dance expression. We introduce the KinoGroove design space, which formalizes how EMG signals can function as primary or complementary control parameters for musical output. Through two usage scenarios, one focusing on large-amplitude movements and the other on low-amplitude muscular activation, we illustrate how the system enables artists to shape sound through both spatial displacement and muscular intensity. We finally describe methods for synchronized acquisition, processing, and visualization of motion and EMG data.
en_abstract_s: Extended reality and motion capture technologies have been explored as tools for embodied artistic performance, allowing dancers and musicians to interact with sound within immersive environments. However, motion capture systems primarily track gross motor gestures and solid body segments, which restricts their ability to represent dance styles that rely on muscular activation and subtle movement rather than large gestures. Electromyography (EMG), by contrast, captures muscular activity and can reflect finer body movements and soft tissue deformation that may not produce large-scale motion, yet are widely used in dance to convey expressive sensibility. In this work, we present Kino- Groove, an extended reality interface that integrates EMG with motion capture to support expressive mapping of dance to sound. KinoGroove was developed through discussions with dancers and artists, involving close collaboration for testing, feedback, and data acquisition. Our system allows musicians to generate and control musical structures through both visible motion and internal effort, covering a wider range of dance expression. We introduce the KinoGroove design space, which formalizes how EMG signals can function as primary or complementary control parameters for musical output. Through two usage scenarios, one focusing on large-amplitude movements and the other on low-amplitude muscular activation, we illustrate how the system enables artists to shape sound through both spatial displacement and muscular intensity. We finally describe methods for synchronized acquisition, processing, and visualization of motion and EMG data.

Mathématiques - Numérique Pôle UnivRennes - Biologie-Santé PEPR eNSEMBLE Continuité collaborative du numérique vers l'humain STAPS COLL-STAPS International No No Yes No KinoGroove: Composing with Muscle and Motion in Extended Reality Nathan Salin e741ad3acacd254ab451476da396b598 irisa.fr 1585453 3761761-1585453 https://orcid.org/0009-0009-7632-3758 Ronan Gaugne a5a896d67738cb0551677a004d0107cf irisa.fr ronan-gaugne 16546 2265-16546 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4762-4342 https://www.idref.fr/231298633 Diane Haering 1288033 1029013-1288033 https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4245-3836 Valérie Gouranton f6b6ae2e8144d212b2eb750f8beac4c9 irisa.fr valerie-gouranton 1272 10511-1272 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9351-2747 https://www.idref.fr/196259843 https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=4ByLsOcAAAAJ Florent Berthaut 135128f0f2a08b92f968df5e49deaae8 hitmuri.net florent-berthaut 5118 30980-5118 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1273-0073 https://www.idref.fr/150316445 New Interfaces for Musical Expression (NIME) 2026 London United Kingdom English Multimodal Interaction Creative Collaboration Asynchronous Interaction Spatial Sound Composition Motion Capture Electromyography (EMG) Virtual Reality (VR) Extended Reality(XR) Extended Reality(XR) Virtual Reality (VR) Multimodal Interaction Creative Collaboration Asynchronous Interaction Spatial Sound Composition Motion Capture Electromyography (EMG) Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Extended reality and motion capture technologies have beenexplored as tools for embodied artistic performance, allowingdancers and musicians to interact with sound within immersiveenvironments. However, motion capture systems primarily trackgross motor gestures and solid body segments, which restrictstheir ability to represent dance styles that rely on muscular activationand subtle movement rather than large gestures. Electromyography(EMG), by contrast, captures muscular activity and canreflect finer body movements and soft tissue deformation thatmay not produce large-scale motion, yet are widely used in danceto convey expressive sensibility. In this work, we present Kino-Groove, an extended reality interface that integrates EMG withmotion capture to support expressive mapping of dance to sound.KinoGroove was developed through discussions with dancersand artists, involving close collaboration for testing, feedback,and data acquisition. Our system allows musicians to generateand control musical structures through both visible motion andinternal effort, covering a wider range of dance expression. Weintroduce the KinoGroove design space, which formalizes howEMG signals can function as primary or complementary controlparameters for musical output. Through two usage scenarios,one focusing on large-amplitude movements and the other onlow-amplitude muscular activation, we illustrate how the systemenables artists to shape sound through both spatial displacementand muscular intensity. We finally describe methods for synchronizedacquisition, processing, and visualization of motion andEMG data.

%X Medical 3D imaging allows surgeons to analyse liver anatomy through reconstructed models, supporting planning and decision-making. As these reconstructions enter operating room, it remains unclear how best to integrate them into surgical workflows, and which display modalities are most effective. After mapping current practices through user research, we investigated four approaches to presenting 3D reconstructions in simulated open-liver surgery. Pairs of surgeons diagnose cases and proposed hepatectomy incision lines under four conditions: (1) 3D reconstructions on a 2D monitor, (2) Augmented Reality (AR) overlay on a 2D monitor, (3) AR overlay via head-mounted display (HMD) for the main surgeon and 2D monitor for the assistant, and (4) AR overlay via HMDs for both surgeons. Results showed that the HMD+HMD condition was preferred for supporting anatomical understanding and collaboration. While accuracy was unaffected, findings emphasise the importance of shared perspective for effective teamwork using AR.

Mixed / augmented reality Collaborative interaction Empirical studies in HCI Field studies Augmented Reality 3D Reconstruction Hepatectomy Open Liver Surgery Teamwork Collaboration Field studies Augmented Reality Empirical studies in HCI Collaborative interaction Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Medical 3D imaging allows surgeons to analyse liver anatomy through reconstructed models, supporting planning and decision-making. As these reconstructions enter operating room, it remains unclear how best to integrate them into surgical workflows, and which display modalities are most effective. After mapping current practices through user research, we investigated four approaches to presenting 3D reconstructions in simulated open-liver surgery. Pairs of surgeons diagnose cases and proposed hepatectomy incision lines under four conditions: (1) 3D reconstructions on a 2D monitor, (2) Augmented Reality (AR) overlay on a 2D monitor, (3) AR overlay via head-mounted display (HMD) for the main surgeon and 2D monitor for the assistant, and (4) AR overlay via HMDs for both surgeons. Results showed that the HMD+HMD condition was preferred for supporting anatomical understanding and collaboration. While accuracy was unaffected, findings emphasise the importance of shared perspective for effective teamwork using AR.

%0 Unpublished work %T Exploring Behavioral Dynamics to Enhance Collective Intelligence in Virtual Environments %+ Laboratoire d'Innovation Numérique pour les Entreprises et les Apprentissages au service de la Compétitivité des Territoires (LINEACT) %A Lannuzel, Tristan %9 Article dans le cadre d'un Doctoral Consortium à IEEEVR 2025 %I IEEE %P 1556-1557 %8 2025-03 %D 2025 %R 10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00423 %K Feedback %K Transactive Memory System %K Multimodal interaction %K Learning %K Virtual reality %K Collective intelligence %K Collective intelligence Virtual reality Learning Multimodal interaction Transactive Memory System Feedback %Z Cognitive scienceOther publications %X

Collective intelligence (CI) is a predictive measure of a group's ability to perform a wide variety of tasks. It is an essential concept for understanding team dynamics and enhancing team performance. While extensively studied in traditional environments such as faceto-face settings or online interactions, CI remains underexplored in immersive Virtual Reality (VR). This thesis has three goals: (1) to analyze how CI manifests in VR through verbal and non-verbal indicators, (2) to design real-time feedback systems that enhance CI in VR environments, and (3) to apply these findings to immersive educational platforms to improve collaboration and learning.

 Exploring Behavioral Dynamics to Enhance Collective Intelligence in Virtual Environments Tristan Lannuzel 2771cae4b6b27e87ee19ecc9c3adbed4 cesi.fr tristan-lannuzel 1521271 3386815-1521271 https://orcid.org/0009-0002-3392-5838 Tristan LANNUZEL 2771cae4b6b27e87ee19ecc9c3adbed4 cesi.fr 2025-06-05 10:59:04 2026-02-07 05:31:19 2025-07-10 08:58:38 2025-03 2025-06-05 contributor Tristan LANNUZEL 2771cae4b6b27e87ee19ecc9c3adbed4 cesi.fr CCSD hal-05098781 https://hal.science/hal-05098781 lannuzel:hal-05098781 2025, pp.1556-1557. <a target="_blank" href="https://dx.doi.org/10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00423">⟨10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00423⟩</a> 2025, pp.1556-1557. ⟨10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00423⟩ HAL Authorization ANR Hesam CESI : groupe d’Enseignement Supérieur et de Formation Professionnelle - Hesam Groupe d’Enseignement Supérieur et de Formation Professionnelle CESI - GROUPE D'ENSEIGNEMENT SUPERIEUR ET DE FORMATION PROFESSIONNELLE PEPR eNSEMBLE Article dans le cadre d'un Doctoral Consortium à IEEEVR 2025 No Exploring Behavioral Dynamics to Enhance Collective Intelligence in Virtual Environments Tristan Lannuzel 2771cae4b6b27e87ee19ecc9c3adbed4 cesi.fr tristan-lannuzel 1521271 3386815-1521271 https://orcid.org/0009-0002-3392-5838 IEEE 1556-1557 2025-03 10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00423 English Feedback Transactive Memory System Multimodal interaction Learning Virtual reality Collective intelligence Collective intelligence Virtual reality Learning Multimodal interaction Transactive Memory System Feedback Cognitive science Other publications Other publications Other publications

<div><p>Collective intelligence (CI) is a predictive measure of a group's ability to perform a wide variety of tasks. It is an essential concept for understanding team dynamics and enhancing team performance. While extensively studied in traditional environments such as faceto-face settings or online interactions, CI remains underexplored in immersive Virtual Reality (VR). This thesis has three goals: (1) to analyze how CI manifests in VR through verbal and non-verbal indicators, (2) to design real-time feedback systems that enhance CI in VR environments, and (3) to apply these findings to immersive educational platforms to improve collaboration and learning.</p></div>

%0 Conference Proceedings
%T [DC] Detection of an Isolated User’s (Inter)Actions during an Augmented Reality Guided Procedure: an Application in Space Medicine
%+ Equipe Immersive Natural User Interaction team (Lab-STICC_INUIT)
%+ École Nationale d'Ingénieurs de Brest (ENIB)
%A Vickery, Frederick
%Z Brest Métropôle
%Z École Nationale d'Ingénieurs de Brest
%< avec comité de lecture

%P 1590-1591
%8 2025-03-08
%D 2025
%R 10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00440
%K Augmented reality
%K interactions
%K human activity recognition
%K application domain models
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR]Conference papers
%X With recent development of Augmented Reality (AR) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology, many new options have become available to overcome previously encountered problems (accurately detecting human activity, adaptive AR guidance, etc.). So when a collaborative scene between a "distant" user in an isolated location in need of assistance from a "local" user in a control centre is put into question, many different solutions can be considered. A solution could be to provide AR guidance for the isolated user with adjustable instructions from the local user who is immersed in a virtual representation of the isolated user’s environment. This allows the local user to quickly understand the problem and current state of the procedure, enabling them to provide the following steps proactively or to rearrange the steps in the current procedure. During my thesis work, our aim is to study how to detect the interactions of an isolated user, in order to create a more detailed virtual environment, improving the quality and priority of the instructed actions provided by the local user. This research subject has become more feasible with recent developments in the fields of AR technology, Human Activity Recognition (HAR) for lightweight or non-intrusive equipment as well as Application Domain Models to represent the environment using semantic information.

 [DC] Detection of an Isolated User’s (Inter)Actions during an Augmented Reality Guided Procedure: an Application in Space Medicine Frederick Vickery b71181325f92c42030ac7cb13c4cc1a7 gmail.com frederick-vickery 1505320 3447979-1505320 https://orcid.org/0009-0008-7704-1771 https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=Dx7JfQIAAAAJ Frederick VICKERY b71181325f92c42030ac7cb13c4cc1a7 gmail.com Brest Métropôle École Nationale d'Ingénieurs de Brest 2025-06-24 16:03:10 2026-03-14 03:14:12 2025-06-24 18:20:47 2025-03-08 2025-06-24 contributor Frederick VICKERY b71181325f92c42030ac7cb13c4cc1a7 gmail.com CCSD hal-05127501 https://hal.science/hal-05127501 vickery:hal-05127501 <i>2025 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces Abstracts and Workshops (VRW 2025)</i>, Mar 2025, Saint Malo, France. pp.1590-1591, <a target="_blank" href="https://dx.doi.org/10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00440">⟨10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00440⟩</a> 2025 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces Abstracts and Workshops (VRW 2025), Mar 2025, Saint Malo, France. pp.1590-1591, ⟨10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00440⟩ Copyright - All rights reserved Université de Bretagne occidentale - Brest (UBO) CNRS - Centre national de la recherche scientifique Université de Bretagne Sud Ecole Nationale d'Ingénieurs de Brest Laboratoire des sciences et techniques de l'information, de la communication et de la connaissance Institut Polytechnique de Paris composantes instituts telecom ANR Equipe Immersive Natural User Interaction team Pôle Interaction PEPR eNSEMBLE Continuité collaborative du numérique vers l'humain Département de Mathèmatiques IP Paris Département d'Information, de Communication et d'Électronique International No No Yes Yes [DC] Detection of an Isolated User’s (Inter)Actions during an Augmented Reality Guided Procedure: an Application in Space Medicine Frederick Vickery b71181325f92c42030ac7cb13c4cc1a7 gmail.com frederick-vickery 1505320 3447979-1505320 https://orcid.org/0009-0008-7704-1771 https://scholar.google.fr/citations?user=Dx7JfQIAAAAJ 2025 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces Abstracts and Workshops (VRW 2025) 2025-03-08 2025-03-12 Saint Malo France IEEE 2025 IEEE Conference on Virtual Reality and 3D User Interfaces Abstracts and Workshops (VRW) 1590-1591 2025 10.1109/VRW66409.2025.00440 English Augmented reality interactions human activity recognition application domain models Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

With recent development of Augmented Reality (AR) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology, many new options have become available to overcome previously encountered problems (accurately detecting human activity, adaptive AR guidance, etc.). So when a collaborative scene between a "distant" user in an isolated location in need of assistance from a "local" user in a control centre is put into question, many different solutions can be considered. A solution could be to provide AR guidance for the isolated user with adjustable instructions from the local user who is immersed in a virtual representation of the isolated user’s environment. This allows the local user to quickly understand the problem and current state of the procedure, enabling them to provide the following steps proactively or to rearrange the steps in the current procedure. During my thesis work, our aim is to study how to detect the interactions of an isolated user, in order to create a more detailed virtual environment, improving the quality and priority of the instructed actions provided by the local user. This research subject has become more feasible with recent developments in the fields of AR technology, Human Activity Recognition (HAR) for lightweight or non-intrusive equipment as well as Application Domain Models to represent the environment using semantic information.

%0 Conference Proceedings
%T Modelling Asymmetries of Collaborative Hybrid Environments
%T Modélisation des asymétries dans les environnements collaboratifs hybrides
%+ Institut de recherche en informatique de Toulouse (IRIT)
%+ Etude de L’Interaction Personne SystèmE (IRIT-ELIPSE)
%+ Université Toulouse III - Paul Sabatier (UT3)
%+ Interacting with Large Data - LISN (ILDA)
%A Brehault, Victor
%A Dubois, Emmanuel
%A Prouzeau, Arnaud
%A Serrano, Marcos
%< avec comité de lecture
%Z Atelier
%B IHM'24 - 35e Conférence Internationale Francophone sur l'Interaction Humain-Machine
%C Paris, France
%V IHM’24 : Actes étendus de la 35ème conférence Francophone sur l’Interaction Humain-Machine
%8 2024-03-25
%D 2024
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Conference papers
%X Hybrid environments consist of a mix of interactive resources of diverse nature, including large screens, personal devices, headsets, mixed reality systems, etc. Such environments facilitate user tasks in complex contexts, such as decision-making in crisis management, and several spatially distributed hybrid environments can enable remote collaboration. However, differences between remote hybrid environments can have an impact on the quality of collaboration. In this paper, we propose a model for describing hybrid environments that helps identify asymmetries between remote users. This model has not yet been finalized, but we believe that it will provide a better understanding of the problems caused by asymmetry, and will help in the development of solutions.
%X Les environnements hybrides sont constitués d'un mélange de ressources interactives de nature diverse, comprenant des grands écrans, des appareils personnels, des casques, des systèmes de réalité mixte, etc. Ces environnements facilitent les tâches des utilisateurs dans des contextes complexes, comme la prise de décision en gestion de crise, et plusieurs environnements hybrides répartis dans l’espace peuvent permettre une collaboration à distance. Cependant, les différences entre des environnements hybrides distants peuvent avoir un impact sur la qualité de la collaboration. Dans ce papier, nous proposons un modèle de description des environnements hybrides qui aide à identifier les asymétries entres les utilisateurs distants. Ce modèle n’est pas finalisé pour le moment, mais nous pensons qu’il permettra de mieux comprendre les problèmes causés par l’asymétrie, et contribuera à l’élaboration de solutions.
%G English

35e Conférence Internationale Francophone sur l'Interaction Humain-Machine 2024-03-25 2024-03-29 Paris France conferenceOrganizer Association Francophone d’Interaction Humain-Machine (AFIHM) Sorbonne Université IHM’24 : Actes étendus de la 35ème conférence Francophone sur l’Interaction Humain-Machine https://ihm2024.afihm.org/ English Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Hybrid environments consist of a mix of interactive resources of diverse nature, including large screens, personal devices, headsets, mixed reality systems, etc. Such environments facilitate user tasks in complex contexts, such as decision-making in crisis management, and several spatially distributed hybrid environments can enable remote collaboration. However, differences between remote hybrid environments can have an impact on the quality of collaboration. In this paper, we propose a model for describing hybrid environments that helps identify asymmetries between remote users. This model has not yet been finalized, but we believe that it will provide a better understanding of the problems caused by asymmetry, and will help in the development of solutions.

Les environnements hybrides sont constitués d'un mélange de ressources interactives de nature diverse, comprenant des grands écrans, des appareils personnels, des casques, des systèmes de réalité mixte, etc. Ces environnements facilitent les tâches des utilisateurs dans des contextes complexes, comme la prise de décision en gestion de crise, et plusieurs environnements hybrides répartis dans l’espace peuvent permettre une collaboration à distance. Cependant, les différences entre des environnements hybrides distants peuvent avoir un impact sur la qualité de la collaboration. Dans ce papier, nous proposons un modèle de description des environnements hybrides qui aide à identifier les asymétries entres les utilisateurs distants. Ce modèle n’est pas finalisé pour le moment, mais nous pensons qu’il permettra de mieux comprendre les problèmes causés par l’asymétrie, et contribuera à l’élaboration de solutions.

%0 Journal Article
%T Mentor-Guided Learning in Immersive Virtual Environments: The Impact of Visual and Haptic Feedback on Skill Acquisition
%+ Informatique, BioInformatique, Systèmes Complexes (IBISC)
%+ Algerian Higher National School of Computer Science
%A Lebrun, Flavien
%A Simon, Cassandre
%A Boukezzi, Assia
%A Otmane, Samir
%A Chellali, Amine
%< avec comité de lecture
%@ 1077-2626
%J IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
%I Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers
%V 31
%N 5
%P 3547--3557
%8 2025-05
%D 2025
%R 10.1109/TVCG.2025.3549547
%K Immersive learning
%K Remote collaboration
%K Mentorship
%K Augmented feedback
%K Multimodal interactions
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]Journal articles
%X In the early stages of learning a technical skill, trainees require guidance from a mentor through augmented feedbackto develop higher expertise. However, the impact of such feedback and the different modalities used to communicate it remainunderexplored in immersive virtual environments (IVE). This paper presents a study in which 27 participants were divided into threegroups to learn a tool manipulation trajectory in an IVE. Two experimental groups received guidance from an expert using visualand/or haptic augmented feedback, while the control group received no feedback. The results indicate that both experimental groupsshowed significantly greater improvement in tool trajectory performance than the control group from pre- to post-test, with no significantdifferences between them. Analysis of their learning curves revealed similar performance improvements in tool trajectory acrosstrials, outperforming the control group. Additionally, the visual-haptic feedback condition was linked to lower task load in three outof six dimensions of the NASA-TLX and a higher perceived interdependence with the expert's actions. These findings suggest thataugmented feedback from an expert enhances the learning of tool manipulation skills. Although adding haptic feedback did not lead tobetter learning outcomes compared to visual feedback alone, it did enhance the overall user experience. These results offer valuableinsights for designing IVEs that support mentor-trainee interactions through augmented feedback.
%G English

title_s: Mentor-Guided Learning in Immersive Virtual Environments: The Impact of Visual and Haptic Feedback on Skill Acquisition
abstract_s: In the early stages of learning a technical skill, trainees require guidance from a mentor through augmented feedback to develop higher expertise. However, the impact of such feedback and the different modalities used to communicate it remain underexplored in immersive virtual environments (IVE). This paper presents a study in which 27 participants were divided into three groups to learn a tool manipulation trajectory in an IVE. Two experimental groups received guidance from an expert using visual and/or haptic augmented feedback, while the control group received no feedback. The results indicate that both experimental groups showed significantly greater improvement in tool trajectory performance than the control group from pre- to post-test, with no significant differences between them. Analysis of their learning curves revealed similar performance improvements in tool trajectory across trials, outperforming the control group. Additionally, the visual-haptic feedback condition was linked to lower task load in three out of six dimensions of the NASA-TLX and a higher perceived interdependence with the expert's actions. These findings suggest that augmented feedback from an expert enhances the learning of tool manipulation skills. Although adding haptic feedback did not lead to better learning outcomes compared to visual feedback alone, it did enhance the overall user experience. These results offer valuable insights for designing IVEs that support mentor-trainee interactions through augmented feedback.

Analysis of their learning curves revealed similar performance improvements in tool trajectory across trials, outperforming the control group. In the early stages of learning a technical skill, trainees require guidance from a mentor through augmented feedbackto develop higher expertise. However, the impact of such feedback and the different modalities used to communicate it remainunderexplored in immersive virtual environments (IVE). This paper presents a study in which 27 participants were divided into threegroups to learn a tool manipulation trajectory in an IVE. Two experimental groups received guidance from an expert using visualand/or haptic augmented feedback, while the control group received no feedback. The results indicate that both experimental groupsshowed significantly greater improvement in tool trajectory performance than the control group from pre- to post-test, with no significantdifferences between them. Analysis of their learning curves revealed similar performance improvements in tool trajectory acrosstrials, outperforming the control group. Additionally, the visual-haptic feedback condition was linked to lower task load in three outof six dimensions of the NASA-TLX and a higher perceived interdependence with the expert's actions. These findings suggest thataugmented feedback from an expert enhances the learning of tool manipulation skills. Although adding haptic feedback did not lead tobetter learning outcomes compared to visual feedback alone, it did enhance the overall user experience. These results offer valuableinsights for designing IVEs that support mentor-trainee interactions through augmented feedback.

%0 Conference Proceedings %T Vers l'amélioration de l'intelligence collective dans un environnement virtuel %+ Laboratoire d'Innovation Numérique pour les Entreprises et les Apprentissages au service de la Compétitivité des Territoires (LINEACT) %A Lannuzel, Tristan %A Biancardi, Beatrice %A Barange, Mukesh %A Buisine, Stéphanie %< avec comité de lecture %B WACAI 2024 - Workshop sur les “Affects, Compagnons Artificiels et Interactions” %C Bordeaux, France %3 WACAI 2024 - Workshop sur les “Affects, Compagnons Artificiels et Interactions” %8 2024-06-12 %D 2024 %K Système de mémoire transactive %K Apprentissage %K Éducation %K Interaction multimodale %K Réalité virtuelle %K Intelligence collective %Z Computer Science [cs] %Z Life Sciences [q-bio]/Neurons and Cognition [q-bio.NC]/Cognitive SciencesConference papers %X

L'intelligence collective est une mesure prédictive de la capacité d'un groupe à accomplir une grande variété de tâches. C'est un indicateur important à prendre en compte pour le fonctionnement efficace d'un groupe. Plusieurs études ont examiné la fiabilité de cet indicateur dans divers environnements, notamment en condition de face-à-face, à travers une communication à distance, et même dans un jeu vidéo en ligne. Cependant, peu d'études se sont intéressées à l'applicabilité de ce concept à la collaboration en réalité virtuelle et, par conséquent, l'influence que ce médium peut avoir sur la dynamique de groupe. À travers le cadre théorique du Système de Mémoire Transactive pour l'Intelligence Collective, l'objectif de ce travail est triple : (1) comprendre comment l'intelligence collective influence le comportement d'un groupe (c.-à-d. les signaux verbaux et non verbaux) dans un environnement virtuel immersif, (2) utiliser ces signaux pour développer une interaction pouvant améliorer la dynamique de groupe en réalité virtuelle, et (3) appliquer ces résultats à une plateforme immersive à vocation pédagogique améliorant la collaboration et l'apprentissage en groupe. Ce travail aura des implications sur les connaissances théoriques concernant l'intelligence collective, ainsi que sur les possibilités offertes par un environnement immersif dans un contexte éducatif.

Cependant, peu d'études se sont intéressées à l'applicabilité de ce concept à la collaboration en réalité virtuelle et, par conséquent, l'influence que ce médium peut avoir sur la dynamique de groupe. À travers le cadre théorique du Système de Mémoire Transactive pour l'Intelligence Collective, l'objectif de ce travail est triple : (1) comprendre comment l'intelligence collective influence le comportement d'un groupe (c.-à-d. les signaux verbaux et non verbaux) dans un environnement virtuel immersif, (2) utiliser ces signaux pour développer une interaction pouvant améliorer la dynamique de groupe en réalité virtuelle, et (3) appliquer ces résultats à une plateforme immersive à vocation pédagogique améliorant la collaboration et l'apprentissage en groupe. Ce travail aura des implications sur les connaissances théoriques concernant l'intelligence collective, ainsi que sur les possibilités offertes par un environnement immersif dans un contexte éducatif.
fr_abstract_s: L'intelligence collective est une mesure prédictive de la capacité d'un groupe à accomplir une grande variété de tâches. C'est un indicateur important à prendre en compte pour le fonctionnement efficace d'un groupe. Plusieurs études ont examiné la fiabilité de cet indicateur dans divers environnements, notamment en condition de face-à-face, à travers une communication à distance, et même dans un jeu vidéo en ligne. Cependant, peu d'études se sont intéressées à l'applicabilité de ce concept à la collaboration en réalité virtuelle et, par conséquent, l'influence que ce médium peut avoir sur la dynamique de groupe. À travers le cadre théorique du Système de Mémoire Transactive pour l'Intelligence Collective, l'objectif de ce travail est triple : (1) comprendre comment l'intelligence collective influence le comportement d'un groupe (c.-à-d. les signaux verbaux et non verbaux) dans un environnement virtuel immersif, (2) utiliser ces signaux pour développer une interaction pouvant améliorer la dynamique de groupe en réalité virtuelle, et (3) appliquer ces résultats à une plateforme immersive à vocation pédagogique améliorant la collaboration et l'apprentissage en groupe. Ce travail aura des implications sur les connaissances théoriques concernant l'intelligence collective, ainsi que sur les possibilités offertes par un environnement immersif dans un contexte éducatif.

Workshop sur les “Affects, Compagnons Artificiels et Interactions” 2024-06-12 2024-06-14 Bordeaux France WACAI 2024 - Workshop sur les “Affects, Compagnons Artificiels et Interactions” French Système de mémoire transactive Apprentissage Éducation Interaction multimodale Réalité virtuelle Intelligence collective Computer Science [cs] Life Sciences [q-bio]/Neurons and Cognition [q-bio.NC]/Cognitive Sciences Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

<div><p>L'intelligence collective est une mesure prédictive de la capacité d'un groupe à accomplir une grande variété de tâches. C'est un indicateur important à prendre en compte pour le fonctionnement efficace d'un groupe. Plusieurs études ont examiné la fiabilité de cet indicateur dans divers environnements, notamment en condition de face-à-face, à travers une communication à distance, et même dans un jeu vidéo en ligne. Cependant, peu d'études se sont intéressées à l'applicabilité de ce concept à la collaboration en réalité virtuelle et, par conséquent, l'influence que ce médium peut avoir sur la dynamique de groupe. À travers le cadre théorique du Système de Mémoire Transactive pour l'Intelligence Collective, l'objectif de ce travail est triple : (1) comprendre comment l'intelligence collective influence le comportement d'un groupe (c.-à-d. les signaux verbaux et non verbaux) dans un environnement virtuel immersif, (2) utiliser ces signaux pour développer une interaction pouvant améliorer la dynamique de groupe en réalité virtuelle, et (3) appliquer ces résultats à une plateforme immersive à vocation pédagogique améliorant la collaboration et l'apprentissage en groupe. Ce travail aura des implications sur les connaissances théoriques concernant l'intelligence collective, ainsi que sur les possibilités offertes par un environnement immersif dans un contexte éducatif.</p></div>

%0 Conference Proceedings
%T Can We Infer Object Pose Changes from Hand Movements?
%+ Interacting with Large Data - LISN (ILDA)
%A Berry, Julien
%A Pietriga, Emmanuel
%A Chapuis, Olivier
%A Appert, Caroline
%< avec comité de lecture
%@ 979-8-4007-2278-3
%( Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
%B CHI 2026 - Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems
%C Barcelone, Spain
%I ACM
%P 16 pages
%8 2026-04-13
%D 2026
%R 10.1145/3772318.3790384
%K Augmented Reality
%K Hand Tracking
%K Object Tracking
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC]
%Z Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR]Conference papers
%X Some Augmented Reality applications require tracking physical objects to anchor virtual elements to them. Despite significant progress in computer vision, achieving robust tracking of manipulated objects remains challenging, notably due to occlusions caused by the hands. Yet the hands carry valuable information: the way an object is grasped and moved is reflected in their shape and motion. We explore the following question: can we infer changes to an object's position and orientation from the shape and movements of the hand manipulating it? We investigate this general approach, which we call Object-from-Hand (ObHa), building three probes, drawing on insights from experimental psychology research on grasping, on our own empirical studies, and on an analysis of the extensive HOT3D dataset of everyday object manipulations. We then discuss the approach's potential either as a complement to vision-based pose tracking solutions or as a coarse standalone pose tracking solution.

title_s: Can We Infer Object Pose Changes from Hand Movements?
abstract_s: Some Augmented Reality applications require tracking physical objects to anchor virtual elements to them. Despite significant progress in computer vision, achieving robust tracking of manipulated objects remains challenging, notably due to occlusions caused by the hands. Yet the hands carry valuable information: the way an object is grasped and moved is reflected in their shape and motion. We explore the following question: can we infer changes to an object's position and orientation from the shape and movements of the hand manipulating it? We investigate this general approach, which we call Object-from-Hand (ObHa), building three probes, drawing on insights from experimental psychology research on grasping, on our own empirical studies, and on an analysis of the extensive HOT3D dataset of everyday object manipulations. We then discuss the approach's potential either as a complement to vision-based pose tracking solutions or as a coarse standalone pose tracking solution.
en_abstract_s: Some Augmented Reality applications require tracking physical objects to anchor virtual elements to them. Despite significant progress in computer vision, achieving robust tracking of manipulated objects remains challenging, notably due to occlusions caused by the hands. We explore the following question: can we infer changes to an object's position and orientation from the shape and movements of the hand manipulating it? We investigate this general approach, which we call Object-from-Hand (ObHa), building three probes, drawing on insights from experimental psychology research on grasping, on our own empirical studies, and on an analysis of the extensive HOT3D dataset of everyday object manipulations. Julien Berry e125cd520e80fa92ccc7e72d6721d692 universite-paris-saclay.fr julien-berry 1474993 3022596-1474993 https://orcid.org/0009-0003-2034-4820 Emmanuel Pietriga ae7da4ca2e73c2e372ff6d6df8119996 inria.fr epietrig 3227 14292-3227 https://arxiv.org/a/pietriga_e_1 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9762-0462 https://www.idref.fr/070320179 http://isni.org/isni/0000000359486503 https://viaf.org/viaf/24861208 Olivier Chapuis 6b2e85c13f6502b1f208ca753d4bc8e5 lri.fr olivier-chapuis 110 14291-110 https://www.idref.fr/18584605X https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0632-3700 Caroline Appert 4c01f2531bbb5405672f59a85954f9b4 universite-paris-saclay.fr caroline-appert 4933 10384-4933 https://www.idref.fr/116215135 https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3050-9284 https://viaf.org/viaf/192918935 http://isni.org/isni/0000000356959918 979-8-4007-2278-3 Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems CHI 2026 - Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems 2026-04-13 2026-04-17 Barcelone Spain ACM 16 pages 10.1145/3772318.3790384 https://chi2026.acm.org/ English Augmented Reality Hand Tracking Object Tracking Computer Science [cs]/Human-Computer Interaction [cs.HC] Computer Science [cs]/Graphics [cs.GR] Conference papers Conference papers Conference papers

Some Augmented Reality applications require tracking physical objects to anchor virtual elements to them. Despite significant progress in computer vision, achieving robust tracking of manipulated objects remains challenging, notably due to occlusions caused by the hands. Yet the hands carry valuable information: the way an object is grasped and moved is reflected in their shape and motion. We explore the following question: can we infer changes to an object's position and orientation from the shape and movements of the hand manipulating it? We investigate this general approach, which we call Object-from-Hand (ObHa), building three probes, drawing on insights from experimental psychology research on grasping, on our own empirical studies, and on an analysis of the extensive HOT3D dataset of everyday object manipulations. We then discuss the approach's potential either as a complement to vision-based pose tracking solutions or as a coarse standalone pose tracking solution.
